findings.subsystem: relayer-fulfillment
findings.title: Transient worker HTTP 5xx/429 is encoded as a permanent on-chain failure callback instead of being retried
findings.evidence: workers/morpheus-relayer/src/nitro.js:91 — callNitro returns {ok:false, status} for ANY HTTP response (only network/timeout throws, line 109). In prepareOracleFulfillment (fulfillment.js:728) the workerResponse is passed straight to encodeFulfillmentResult (router.js:586-594), which on !ok produces {success:false, error:'worker request failed with status 503'}. processEvent then delivers that as the on-chain fulfillRequest(success=false). Once it lands, fulfilledAtMs is set and the request is terminal (neo-n3.js:261-263 alreadySettled guard -> never re-scanned). So a temporarily-overloaded enclave, a rate-limited upstream price provider (429), or a transient 502/503/504 from the worker is irreversibly burned into a hard failure callback to the consumer contract — even though the SAME network-level error, had callNitro thrown it, would be classified transient (fulfillment.js:90-95) and retried. The retry/backoff machinery only protects network-level and on-chain-level transients, not worker-response-level transients. Decrypt lane has the same defect (fulfillment.js:682-696).
findings.fix: In prepareOracleFulfillment, before calling encodeFulfillmentResult, treat a transient worker HTTP status as a thrown transient error so the existing retry path handles it: if workerResponse is !ok AND the status is retryable (429, 408, 425, 500, 502, 503, 504, or 0), throw new Error(`worker request failed with status ${workerResponse.status} (transient)`) — the 'transient'/status keywords route it through classifyError -> scheduleRetry instead of finalizing. Keep deterministic worker failures (400/401/403/404/409/422) on the deliver-as-failure path. Apply the same status gate to the /oracle/decrypt branch (fulfillment.js:682). Add a unit test asserting a 503 worker response retries while a 400 finalizes.
findings.risk: Mis-classifying a genuinely-permanent worker failure as transient would delay the on-chain failure callback by the retry ceiling instead of delivering it immediately; bounded by resolveCallbackRetryCeiling so it still dead-letters/finalizes eventually. Net strictly safer than the current burn-on-first-transient behavior.

findings.subsystem: relayer-fulfillment
findings.title: Same on-chain request gets two different event keys across discovery paths, causing cross-tick double-processing in default mainnet block_cursor mode
findings.evidence: buildEventKey (state.js:135-143) hashes chain:requestId:txHash:logIndex:blockNumber. The notification/block-scan path sets a real txHash+logIndex+blockNumber (neo-n3.js:359-361), but the request-cursor scan used by reconcilePendingRequests (scanNeoN3OracleRequestsById -> buildNeoN3EventFromRequestRecord) sets txHash='' , logIndex=0, blockNumber=createdAtMs (neo-n3.js:278-280, 316-318). These produce DIFFERENT keys for the same requestId. Mainnet default scanMode is 'block_cursor' (config.js:444-446), which runs processChain: a fresh request found by block scan gets key K1 (with txHash); if its worker call fails transiently it sits in retry_queue under K1. reconcilePendingRequests only excludes requestIds observed in the SAME tick's block scan (request-processor.js:89-90, observedRequestIds), so on a later tick the same request is re-scanned by id with key K2 (txHash=''). filterNewEvents (request-processor.js:47-67) dedups by key, not requestId, and isEventQueuedForRetry(K2)/hasProcessedEvent(K2) are both false -> the request is processed a SECOND time under K2 while K1's retry item still exists. The durable cross-instance claim does not help (durable jobs are keyed by event_key too, so K1 and K2 are separate jobs).
findings.fix: Make discovery dedup requestId-aware in block_cursor mode. Cheapest correct fix: in reconcilePendingRequests, exclude any requestId already present in state[chain].retry_queue or processed_records (compare by String(event.requestId)), not only this-tick observedRequestIds — e.g. build a Set of retry/processed requestIds and add it to the pendingOnly filter at request-processor.js:89. Alternatively, stabilize buildEventKey to key purely on chain:requestId for the relayer (txHash/logIndex/blockNumber are not needed for idempotency since a request settles once) — but that is a broader change touching durable job keys, so the requestId-aware reconcile filter is the lower-risk fix.
findings.risk: On-chain idempotency ('already fulfilled' -> classifyError settled) prevents an actual double callback, so today this manifests as wasted worker calls (and for random.generate a different unused 32-byte value computed each time), duplicate retry-queue/durable entries that never converge, and inflated metrics — not fund loss. The fix must keep reconciliation able to pick up requests the block scan genuinely missed (filter by retry/processed membership, not by 'seen before at all').

findings.subsystem: relayer-fulfillment
findings.title: processEvent remains a ~390-line function with a ~338-line try/catch despite the helper extraction
findings.evidence: fulfillment.js:1006-1398. The scheduleCallbackRetry / recordTerminalOutcome / recordDeliveryExhaustion helpers are extracted and used, but processEvent's catch block still inlines five distinct outcome arms (already-fulfilled, terminal-config, prepared-redelivery ceiling+retry, finalize-only ceiling+retry, and the primary scheduleRetry->exhausted->finalize->nested-catch) totaling ~338 lines, including a fully duplicated finalize-success recording block (1271-1315) that re-implements the happy-path recordProcessedEvent/maybeUpsertJob/log sequence from 1094-1136 with slightly different fields. The nested finalize catch (1316-1360) duplicates the prepared/finalize ceiling logic a fourth time. This is the GAS-callback delivery money path and the duplication is where terminal-vs-transient drift will silently creep in.
findings.fix: Extract the 'exhausted -> finalizeFailedRequest -> record failed' success block (1271-1315) into a recordFinalizedFailure(config,state,persistState,logger,event,kernelIntent,{attempts,result,requestAgeMs}) helper reusing the same recorder shape as the happy path. Collapse the two ceiling-or-retry decision blocks (prepared at 1186-1223, finalize-only at 1225-1260, nested-finalize at 1316-1360) into one resolveDeliveryRetryOrExhaust(...) helper that takes the route/extraRetryItemFields/upsert* and decides recordDeliveryExhaustion vs scheduleCallbackRetry. Target processEvent <150 lines as a dispatcher. The 4 end-to-end ceiling tests (fulfillment.test.mjs:483-586) pin behavior; do behavior-preserving extraction and keep them green.
findings.risk: Behavior-preserving refactor of the live callback-delivery path; the existing ceiling/exhaustion tests are the gate. No observable behavior should change.

findings.subsystem: relayer-fulfillment
findings.title: Redelivery worker_response loses the kernel_intent enrichment that the fresh-success path adds
findings.evidence: On the happy path, maybeUpsertJob enriches worker_response with kernel_intent {module_id, operation, legacy_request_type} (fulfillment.js:1122-1132). But when a callback retry succeeds via the preparedForRedelivery branch (fulfillment.js:1065-1067), result.worker_response is the prepared.worker_response carried in the retry item and the final success upsert still runs through the same block — however the prepared redelivery path stores buildCallbackPendingWorkerResponse (1216) on the scheduled retry, which wraps the original worker_response under retry_meta. After a successful redelivery the durable job's worker_response therefore reflects the retry_meta-wrapped shape rather than the clean kernel_intent-tagged shape a first-try success would have, making dead-letter/replay inspection of redelivered-then-succeeded jobs inconsistent with first-try jobs.
findings.fix: When building the final success upsert (fulfillment.js:1116-1136), normalize result.worker_response by unwrapping a retry_meta wrapper if present (prefer result.worker_response.retry_meta?.prepared_fulfillment?.worker_response ?? result.worker_response) before applying the kernel_intent enrichment, so redelivered successes record the same worker_response shape as first-try successes. Add an assertion in the redelivery-success test.
findings.risk: Observability/consistency only; no delivery-correctness impact. Verify the dead-letter/replay UI reads worker_response by the unwrapped shape.

findings.subsystem: relayer-fulfillment
findings.title: events_processed_total / fulfill_* metrics are incremented per redelivery attempt, double-counting a single logical request
findings.evidence: fulfillment.js:1067, 1090, 1092 — every pass through processEvent that reaches delivery increments fulfill_success_total/fulfill_failure_total and events_processed_total. A request whose callback delivery is retried N times (prepared-redelivery branch) therefore contributes N to events_processed_total and up to N-1 fulfill_failure_total + 1 fulfill_success_total for one logical request. worker_calls_total is correctly NOT re-incremented on redelivery (it sits inside the fresh-prepare branch at 1069), so worker_calls_total and events_processed_total diverge in a way that makes 'processed vs worker-called' ratios misleading on any chain with delivery retries.
findings.fix: Increment events_processed_total once per terminal outcome (fulfilled/failed/settled/exhausted) rather than once per delivery attempt — e.g. move it next to the recordProcessedEvent('fulfilled'/'failed') calls and skip it on the scheduleCallbackRetry path. Alternatively add a distinct delivery_attempts_total metric and reserve events_processed_total for terminal transitions. Document the intended semantics in state.js defaultMetrics.
findings.risk: Metric-only; ensure any dashboards/alerts keyed on events_processed_total are updated for the new (lower, more accurate) counts.

findings.subsystem: relayer-queue-state (workers/morpheus-relayer/src/queue.js + state.js + persistence.js)
findings.title: Backoff-skipped retry items are deleted from local state and lost during a multi-instance Supabase outage
findings.evidence: fulfillment.js:1039-1051: when claimDurableJobForProcessing returns false, processEvent unconditionally calls clearRetryItem(state, chain, eventKey) and returns 'claimed_elsewhere'. But that helper returns false for TWO different reasons: (a) a real cross-instance conflict (queue.js:382-392) and (b) the backoff-skip when allowLocalClaimDuringBackoff=false (queue.js:316-324) where NO instance processed the job. For case (b), the item is a prepared callback already sitting in the local retry_queue (e.g. a 'callback_pending' redelivery). clearRetryItem drops it from local state, but because Supabase is down, hydrateDurableQueue (queue.js:395-399 returns [] under shouldSkipSupabasePersistence) cannot bring it back during the outage. The work is silently dropped until Supabase recovers AND the durable row still carries a ready status — and the durable row may itself be stale because the last patch happened before the outage. This is exactly the multi-instance config the option was built for (config.js:332-335).
findings.fix: In processEvent (fulfillment.js:1040), distinguish a true cross-instance conflict from a backoff-skip. Have claimDurableJobForProcessing return a discriminated result (e.g. {granted:false, reason:'conflict'|'backoff_skip'}) instead of a bare boolean. On 'backoff_skip', do NOT clearRetryItem — leave the item in retry_queue with next_retry_at bumped by a short delay so it is retried on a later tick once Supabase recovers, and do NOT increment claim_conflicts_total. Only clearRetryItem on a genuine 'conflict'.
findings.risk: Multi-instance deploys (allowLocalClaimDuringBackoff=false) lose already-prepared oracle callbacks during a Supabase outage — a lost/under-delivered callback class of fault with no in-outage recovery. Single-instance default is unaffected (claim is granted), which is why this has not bitten yet.

findings.subsystem: relayer-queue-state (workers/morpheus-relayer/src/queue.js + state.js + persistence.js)
findings.title: claim_conflicts_total conflates real cross-instance conflicts with Supabase-backoff skips
findings.evidence: fulfillment.js:1041 increments claim_conflicts_total whenever claimDurableJobForProcessing returns false. queue.js:316-324 returns false for the allowLocalClaimDuringBackoff=false backoff-skip path. So during a Supabase outage a multi-instance relayer reports a flood of 'claim conflicts' that are not conflicts at all — masking the real signal (another instance genuinely won the claim) and creating a misleading alert during an outage.
findings.fix: Only increment claim_conflicts_total for the genuine cross-instance-conflict return (queue.js:382-392). The backoff-skip path already has its own metric (durable_claim_skipped_during_backoff_total, queue.js:316); leave conflict counting to the true-conflict branch once claimDurableJobForProcessing returns a discriminated reason (pairs with the previous finding).
findings.risk: On-call misreads a Supabase outage as a cross-instance double-claim storm; the metric meant to detect split-brain becomes unreliable.

findings.subsystem: relayer-queue-state (workers/morpheus-relayer/src/queue.js + state.js + persistence.js)
findings.title: Three incremented relayer metrics are never exported to /metrics and never seeded in defaultMetrics()
findings.evidence: request-processor.js:189,288 increments discovery_failures_total; :211 increments reconciliation_failures_total; queue.js:316 increments durable_claim_skipped_during_backoff_total. None of the three are in state.js defaultMetrics() (lines 28-60) nor in prometheus.js topLevelCounters (lines 23-44). metrics-server.js:11 shows renderPrometheusMetrics is the ENTIRE observability surface (the only HTTP endpoint), so these counters live only in the in-memory/state JSON and are invisible to Prometheus/alerting. incrementMetric (state.js:159-164) lazily creates the key so no crash, but the values never reach a dashboard. These are precisely the signals you want to alert on: RPC discovery failing every tick, and idempotency protection being OFF during a Supabase backoff.
findings.fix: Add discovery_failures_total, reconciliation_failures_total, durable_claim_skipped_during_backoff_total (and the already-incremented stale_reclaims_total is present, but verify) to state.js defaultMetrics() so they survive restart at 0, and append them to prometheus.js topLevelCounters so they are scraped. Add a unit test asserting every incrementMetric() call-site name appears in topLevelCounters to prevent future drift.
findings.risk: Operators cannot see or alert on discovery-RPC failure rate or on the window where cross-instance idempotency is disabled — the latter is the highest-stakes signal in this subsystem and is currently unobservable.

findings.subsystem: relayer-queue-state (workers/morpheus-relayer/src/queue.js + state.js + persistence.js)
findings.title: Non-quota Supabase outages (503/timeout/ECONNRESET) never trip the backoff window — every operation keeps paying the 15s timeout each tick
findings.evidence: persistence.js:91-92 markSupabasePersistenceUnavailable only arms the backoff when isSupabaseQuotaRestrictedError(error) is true, which (persistence.js:80-89) matches ONLY quota/402 strings. A plain outage (503/502/504, ECONNRESET, ECONNREFUSED, 'timed out', 'unavailable') is recognized by isTransientDurableQueueError (queue.js:250-272) for per-call local fallback, but never sets supabasePersistenceBackoffUntilMs. So shouldSkipSupabasePersistence() stays false and every maybeUpsertJob / claimRelayerJob / hydrateDurableQueue / quarantine call still issues a request that hangs up to AbortSignal.timeout(15_000) (persistence.js:163) before falling back. With several Supabase calls per event per tick, a non-quota outage inflates tick latency by tens of seconds while the quota path correctly fast-fails.
findings.fix: When isTransientDurableQueueError(error) matches a connectivity/5xx/timeout class (not just quota), also arm a (shorter) backoff window via markSupabasePersistenceUnavailable with a distinct reason, so subsequent calls in the same tick short-circuit via shouldSkipSupabasePersistence. Keep the quota window length; use a shorter window (e.g. 30s) for transient connectivity so recovery is fast. Add a test mirroring queue.test.mjs:191 for a 503 error.
findings.risk: During a Supabase network/5xx outage each relayer tick stalls for many seconds of cumulative 15s timeouts, slowing oracle callback delivery for live requests even though local state would let it make progress immediately.

findings.subsystem: relayer-queue-state (workers/morpheus-relayer/src/queue.js + state.js + persistence.js)
findings.title: retry_queue has no size ceiling — unlike processed_records and dead_letters it can grow unbounded in the persisted state file
findings.evidence: state.js bounds processed_records via pruneProcessedRecords (limits.processedCacheSize, default 5000) and dead_letters via pruneDeadLetters (limits.deadLetterLimit, default 500), both applied in recordProcessedEvent (state.js:275-277). retry_queue has NO equivalent cap: enqueueRetryItem (state.js:214-241) and scheduleRetry (state.js:281-324) only push/replace. The only shrink paths are success/exhaustion (clearRetryItem) and pruneRetryQueueBelowRequestFloor (chain-cursor.js, only when startRequestIds floor is set). deferEventsForBackpressure (queue.js:107-120) enqueues the entire deferred TAIL as retry items every overflow tick. Under a sustained burst that outruns maxFreshEventsPerTick (config default 32) combined with a downstream worker/RPC failure (so items never clear), retry_queue grows without bound; the whole array is re-serialized to the state file on every persist (state.js:131, JSON.stringify of full state) — O(n) write per persist plus an ever-larger on-disk snapshot.
findings.fix: Add a configurable retry_queue ceiling (e.g. MORPHEUS_RELAYER_RETRY_QUEUE_LIMIT) enforced in enqueueRetryItem/scheduleRetry: when exceeded, dead-letter the oldest/lowest-priority items (push to dead_letters with a 'retry_queue_overflow' reason and emit a metric) rather than growing without bound. Surface retry_queue_sizes (already in snapshotMetrics) with an alert threshold.
findings.risk: Persisted state file and per-persist serialization cost grow unbounded under a sustained-overflow + downstream-failure combination, degrading every tick and eventually risking disk/memory pressure on the box. No automatic shedding.

findings.subsystem: relayer-queue-state (workers/morpheus-relayer/src/queue.js + state.js + persistence.js)
findings.title: Each dead-letter stores the full raw event payload with only a count-based cap, so the bound is on entries not bytes
findings.evidence: state.js:264-274 pushes a dead-letter record that embeds the entire event object (`event,`) plus meta; pruneDeadLetters (state.js:153-157) caps by COUNT (default 500) not by serialized size. Oracle events for the decrypt-seal / http / compute lanes can carry large encrypted_inputs / payload blobs, so 500 full events can make the dead_letters slice (and the persisted state file, re-serialized whole on each write at state.js:131) disproportionately large relative to its operational value — the dead-letter only needs key/request_id/request_type/route/error to drive manual replay (the full event can be re-fetched from the durable Supabase row, which already stores it).
findings.fix: Store only the identifying fields + error in the local dead_letters entry (key, request_id, request_type, module_id, operation, chain, last_error, exhausted_at) and drop the embedded full `event`; manual replay already reads the full event from the Supabase job row (queue.js:156-178 reads job.event). If the event must stay for offline replay, add a byte-size-aware prune in addition to the count cap.
findings.risk: Larger-than-necessary state file inflates every atomic save and load; mostly a hygiene/perf-tax issue, not a correctness bug.

findings.subsystem: relayer-queue-state (workers/morpheus-relayer/src/queue.js + state.js + persistence.js)
findings.title: deferEventsForBackpressure persists each deferred event to Supabase with await inside a serial loop, blocking the tick under burst+backoff
findings.evidence: queue.js:107-120: the deferred-event loop does `await maybeUpsertJob(...)` once per deferred event serially. maybeUpsertJob short-circuits under shouldSkipSupabasePersistence (queue.js:68), so during an armed quota backoff this is cheap — but during a non-quota outage (which, per the separate finding, does NOT arm the backoff) each maybeUpsertJob makes a real request that can hang up to 15s (persistence.js:163), serialized across potentially hundreds of deferred events. persistFreshEventsToDurableQueue already uses mapWithConcurrency (queue.js:284) for the analogous fresh-event insert; the deferral path does not.
findings.fix: Batch the deferred-event upserts (single multi-row POST) or run them through mapWithConcurrency like persistFreshEventsToDurableQueue, and guard the whole loop with a single shouldSkipSupabasePersistence check (it already is, but pair with the non-quota-backoff fix so outages short-circuit). Local enqueueRetryItem state is the source of truth; the durable mirror can be best-effort/batched.
findings.risk: Under a large burst that triggers backpressure deferral during a non-quota Supabase outage, the tick serializes many 15s-timeout requests, compounding the latency hit from the broader non-quota-backoff gap.

findings.subsystem: relayer-chain-scan (workers/morpheus-relayer/src: neo-n3.js, neox.js, request-processor.js, chain-cursor.js, state.js)
findings.title: Neo X (EVM) path has no RPC failover — a single dead RPC endpoint kills the entire neox lane
findings.evidence: neox.js:59-69 getProvider() builds one ethers.JsonRpcProvider from config.neox.rpcUrl, and config.js:511-516 resolves neox.rpcUrl as a single string (no neox.rpcUrls array). Every neox operation — getNeoXLatestBlock (neox.js:107), getNeoXLatestRequestId/totalRequests (neox.js:111-114), the per-id getRequest scan (neox.js:234), and fulfillRequest submission (neox.js:391/397) — funnels through that one provider. Contrast the Neo N3 path, which has full failover: neoRpcCall (neo-n3.js:68-115) iterates getNeoN3RpcUrls(config) and rotates to the next URL on any error. So if the configured Neo X RPC is down or rate-limiting, neox discovery AND fulfillment both stall with zero recovery, while the N3 lane tolerates the same failure.
findings.fix: Mirror the N3 multi-URL model on Neo X: add config.neox.rpcUrls (parse MORPHEUS_RELAYER_NEOX_RPC_URLS / comma-list like neoN3RpcUrls in config.js), and in neox.js maintain a provider per URL (the providerCache is already keyed by rpcUrl|chainId) with a small failover wrapper around the read calls (getBlockNumber/totalRequests/getRequest) that tries each URL in order on a transport error. Fulfillment submission can keep a single primary but should fall back on provider-level connection errors. Keep the per-signer submitQueue serialization unchanged.
findings.risk: Neo X is a real production lane (per memory: Neo X mainnet oracle live). A single-RPC dependency is a single point of failure for cross-chain delivery; the asymmetry vs N3 shows the failover pattern is already considered necessary.

findings.subsystem: relayer-chain-scan (workers/morpheus-relayer/src: neo-n3.js, neox.js, request-processor.js, chain-cursor.js, state.js)
findings.title: confirmations.neox is dead config: the request-cursor path applies zero confirmations, so a reorged-out Neo X request is recorded as 'settled' (lost callback)
findings.evidence: config.js:396 sets confirmations.neox=2 (env MORPHEUS_RELAYER_NEOX_CONFIRMATIONS), but request-processor.js:151 is the ONLY place config.confirmations[chain] is read, and that line lives exclusively in processChain (the block_cursor path). The neox lane runs through processChainByRequestCursor (relayer.js:172-179), which reads totalRequests() at the chain tip and immediately scans + fulfills brand-new requests with no confirmation depth (grep confirms confirmations is referenced nowhere in processChainByRequestCursor / neox.js). Consequence on a probabilistic-finality chain: the relayer can fulfill a request whose creating tx later reorgs out; the fulfillRequest then reverts RequestNotPending, normalizeNeoXRevert (neox.js:318) maps it to 'request already fulfilled', and isAlreadyFulfilledError (fulfillment.js:53-58) classifies it as terminal 'settled' (fulfillment.js:82, 1158-1167) — i.e. recorded as success with no retry, permanently losing a callback that was never actually delivered.
findings.fix: Apply a confirmation gate in processChainByRequestCursor before fulfilling: only process request ids whose creating block is at/below (latestBlock - confirmations[chain]). Practically, add an optional options.getRequestBlock or have the EVM adapter expose createdAt block and require provider.getBlockNumber() - createdAtBlock >= confirmations.neox before fulfilling (defer the rest to a later tick via the cursor not advancing past them). At minimum, if confirmations are intentionally not enforced for request_cursor, remove the misleading confirmations.neox config and document that neox fulfils at 0-conf — but the safer fix is to honor it.
findings.risk: Either a real lost-callback vector under Neo X reorgs, or (if Neo X finality is effectively instant) a configuration trap where operators set NEOX_CONFIRMATIONS expecting safety they don't get. The reorg-to-'settled' chain is code-certain; the finality probability is the needs-verify part.

findings.subsystem: relayer-chain-scan (workers/morpheus-relayer/src: neo-n3.js, neox.js, request-processor.js, chain-cursor.js, state.js)
findings.title: n3index notification scan uses block_index.desc + limit, silently dropping the OLDEST requests in a high-volume window
findings.evidence: scanNeoN3OracleRequestsViaN3Index (neo-n3.js:455-456) sets order=block_index.desc and limit=max(maxBlocksPerTick*4, 500), then filters client-side to [fromBlock,toBlock] (neo-n3.js:470-474). Because rows are returned newest-first and truncated at the limit, when the number of oracle-request notifications in the scanned window exceeds the limit the rows nearest fromBlock (the oldest) are the ones cut. processChain still advances state.last_block to toBlock unconditionally (request-processor.js:178), so those dropped requests are never re-scanned by the notification path — recovery depends entirely on the request-cursor reconciliation (reconcilePendingRequests), which is itself capped at maxBlocksPerTick ids/tick (request-processor.js:87). This is the testnet DEFAULT scan mode (config.js:446). Under a burst it can defer (not lose, but delay) callbacks behind the reconciliation backlog.
findings.fix: Query ascending (order=block_index.asc,notification_index.asc) so truncation drops the NEWEST overflow rows (which will be re-fetched next tick as the cursor advances) instead of the oldest, OR add a notification_index secondary sort and page until the window is exhausted, OR cap the scanned window so block_index range * expected-density stays under the limit. Ascending order is the minimal, behavior-preserving fix (the code already re-sorts ascending afterward at neo-n3.js:475-480).
findings.risk: Default testnet path; misses are recoverable via reconciliation but introduce latency and make correctness depend on a second capped subsystem rather than the primary scan.

findings.subsystem: relayer-chain-scan (workers/morpheus-relayer/src: neo-n3.js, neox.js, request-processor.js, chain-cursor.js, state.js)
findings.title: promoteNeoN3RpcUrl permanently reorders the configured RPC list on every successful call, so a slow fallback can demote the operator's preferred primary forever
findings.evidence: neoRpcCall calls promoteNeoN3RpcUrl(configOrRpcUrl, rpcUrl) (neo-n3.js:103-105) on EVERY successful response, which sets config.neo_n3.rpcUrl=nextUrl and moves it to the front of config.neo_n3.rpcUrls (neo-n3.js:54-59). The promotion is unconditional (any success, including routine read calls), and the mutation is sticky for the process lifetime. If the preferred primary (first in MORPHEUS_RELAYER_NEO_N3_RPC_URLS, ranked by rpcUrlRank in config.js:67-76) briefly fails once and a lower-preference fallback answers, that fallback becomes the de-facto primary indefinitely with no re-promotion of the original. There is no health-based demotion back to the configured order.
findings.fix: Only promote when failover actually occurred (i.e. rpcUrl !== the current head), and consider re-trying the configured primary first each tick (sticky-on-failure rather than sticky-on-success). Alternatively keep the configured order immutable and track a separate 'current best' index that resets to the preferred head periodically. Low urgency but removes a silent, sticky preference inversion.
findings.risk: Operators ordering RPCs by cost/latency/trust can end up permanently pinned to a worse endpoint after one transient blip, with no log/metric indicating the inversion.

findings.subsystem: relayer-chain-scan (workers/morpheus-relayer/src: neo-n3.js, neox.js, request-processor.js, chain-cursor.js, state.js)
findings.title: block_cursor scan calls getapplicationlog for EVERY transaction in every scanned block (no contract pre-filter) — large RPC amplification on busy mainnet blocks / catch-up
findings.evidence: scanNeoN3OracleRequestBlock (neo-n3.js:389-419) fetches the block then issues getapplicationlog for transaction.txid of EVERY tx in the block (neo-n3.js:393-396), filtering for the oracle contract only AFTER the log is fetched (neo-n3.js:404). block_cursor is the mainnet default (config.js:446). Per tick the relayer issues 1 getblock + N getapplicationlog where N = total txs across [fromBlock,toBlock], regardless of how many (usually zero) touched the oracle contract. With maxBlocksPerTick=250 (config.js:301) a post-downtime catch-up over a busy range becomes tens of thousands of getapplicationlog calls at concurrency 4 (resolveScanConcurrency default, neo-n3.js:499-502). Batching (already landed in cd1fd8a) improves latency but does NOT reduce the total call count.
findings.fix: Keep per-tx getapplicationlog (correct for Neo N3), but reduce how many txs reach it: (a) make n3index_notifications the mainnet default too (or recommend it in deploy docs) since the indexer already filters by contract_hash + event_name server-side, eliminating the per-tx fan-out entirely; or (b) if an indexer isn't desired, raise default scan concurrency for catch-up and add a metric (e.g. getapplicationlog_calls_total) so the amplification is observable. Do NOT pursue the getblock-returns-notifications shortcut (factually wrong).
findings.risk: Steady-state cost is low (0-2 blocks/tick), so this only bites on backfill after downtime; but during a long outage recovery it can hammer the RPC node and slow the catch-up that matters most. Catch-up-dominated, partially overlapping the roadmap's batching item.

findings.subsystem: relayer-chain-scan (workers/morpheus-relayer/src: neo-n3.js, neox.js, request-processor.js, chain-cursor.js, state.js)
findings.title: Neo X reorg/cursor-reset and RPC-failover behavior is entirely untested
findings.evidence: grep over neox.test.mjs and neo-n3.test.mjs shows no test for failover, reorg, rollback, or confirmation depth on the neox path (only a Neo N3 'confirmation timed out' assertion at neo-n3.test.mjs:516). The neox lane carries the genuinely risky branches: single-RPC dependency (finding above), the 'RequestNotPending -> settled' classification (neox.js:318 + fulfillment.js:1158), the per-signer submitQueue + signer.reset() recovery (neox.js:409-416), and the request-cursor reset on totalRequests shrinking (resolveRequestCursor, request-processor.js:331-343). None of these have an isolated test, so any change to the EVM scan/fulfill path is unverifiable.
findings.fix: Add unit tests with a mock ethers contract/provider for: (1) totalRequests() decreasing between ticks -> resolveRequestCursor resets and does not over-advance; (2) getRequest throwing a transport (non-CALL_EXCEPTION) error -> scan aborts and last_request_id is NOT advanced; (3) CALL_EXCEPTION on one id -> that id skipped, others still scanned in order; (4) fulfillRequest reverting RequestNotPending -> classified terminal 'settled' (pin the current behavior so the confirmations fix above is a deliberate change). These are pure-function-testable via the existing adapter seams.
findings.risk: The EVM money path has the least coverage and the most distinct failure modes; without tests the confirmations/failover fixes can't be landed safely.

findings.subsystem: nitro-worker-feeds (price-feed pipeline: workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/feeds/* + aggregation + deploy/feed-pusher)
findings.title: Production on-chain price feed is single-source (TwelveData only) with no cross-source validation; aggregation engine exists but is not in the on-chain path
findings.evidence: deploy/feed-pusher/feed-pusher.mjs is the systemd unit that actually writes mainnet feeds (morpheus-feed-pusher.service / -neox.service). Its only fetcher is td() (line 123) — TwelveData alone. Every on-chain record is keyed 'TWELVEDATA:<pair>' (feed-pusher.mjs:193,246,257). The multi-source median/trimmed-mean + outlier-rejection engine in workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/aggregation.js and fetchPriceQuotes (feeds.js:388) computes an `aggregation` object but feeds.js:580-655 submits each provider's quote under a SEPARATE storage_pair (getFeedStoragePair = `PROVIDER:PAIR`, feed-registry.js:306), so the aggregated price is never written on-chain — it is only echoed in the HTTP response (feeds.js:751). Net effect: a single bad TwelveData tick (glitch/flash-print/wrong-symbol) lands directly on-chain with no second-source corroboration. Registry even defines binance-spot for NEO-USD/GAS-USD (feed-registry.js:13-20) but those produce a distinct BINANCE-SPOT:* feed nobody reads.
findings.fix: Make the on-chain pusher multi-source for at least the high-value crypto pairs: in feed-pusher.mjs add a second fetcher (binance-spot / coinbase-spot) and aggregate (reuse the median+deviation logic from aggregation.js) before planFeedUpdate, OR move the production push to use nitro-worker's fetchPriceQuotes + aggregation and write ONE storage_pair per logical pair (e.g. 'NEO-USD' instead of 'TWELVEDATA:NEO-USD'). At minimum, write the aggregated price (quoteSet.aggregation.price) to a single canonical storage_pair in handleOracleFeed instead of per-provider keys, and have the pusher cross-check TwelveData against a free second source and refuse to push when they diverge beyond maxDeviationPct.
findings.risk: Changing on-chain storage_pair keys is a breaking change for existing consumers reading 'TWELVEDATA:NEO-USD'; must keep writing the legacy key (or dual-write) during migration. Adding a second source adds an external dependency and a new failure mode to handle (one source down should degrade, not block).

findings.subsystem: nitro-worker-feeds (price-feed pipeline: workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/feeds/* + aggregation + deploy/feed-pusher)
findings.title: Production feed-pusher has no price-sanity / max-deviation guard: an absurd or zero source price is pushed straight on-chain
findings.evidence: deploy/feed-pusher/feed-pusher.mjs planFeedUpdate (line 31-42) only decides push-vs-skip on (a) staleness and (b) a LOWER change threshold (`unchanged` when move < thresholdBps). There is no UPPER bound: a 10x spike or 0.0001x collapse from TwelveData passes through. td() (line 137-143) accepts `v="0"`: `if (v && !isNaN(Number(v)))` — the string "0" is truthy and Number("0")=0 is finite, so a zero price is accepted, then `Math.round(0*1e6)=0` is pushed (line 248). The MorpheusDataFeed contract only asserts `price >= 0` (contracts/MorpheusDataFeed/MorpheusDataFeed.cs:124), so it does not reject the zero/absurd value either. The contract's own comment (cs:183-189) acknowledges this and provides AdminResetFeed as the manual recovery for 'a single bad publish'. The nitro-worker path is partially protected (transformDecimalString throws on numeric<=0, feeds/decimal.js:57) but the production cron path is not.
findings.fix: In feed-pusher.mjs planFeedUpdate, reject (return push:false + log an ALERT) when newPrice <= 0, when !Number.isFinite(newPrice), or when |newPrice-cur.price|/cur.price exceeds a configurable MAX_DEVIATION_BPS (e.g. 2000 = 20%) while cur.price>0 — except when cur is zero/uninitialised (bootstrap) where a large move is expected. Tighten td() to drop v<=0. Add unit tests in feed-pusher.test.mjs mirroring the existing planFeedUpdate cases for the spike/zero/NaN rejection paths.
findings.risk: A legitimate large real move (e.g., a genuine flash event) would be skipped and alert; needs an admin-reviewable override, otherwise a real >MAX_DEVIATION move stalls until a human raises the bound. Keep the bootstrap (cur.price==0) bypass so first-publish still works.

findings.subsystem: nitro-worker-feeds (price-feed pipeline: workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/feeds/* + aggregation + deploy/feed-pusher)
findings.title: Two pipelines write the same on-chain feed with different timestamp sources; an upstream provider timestamp can be older-than or far-future, tripping the contract's monotonic-timestamp guard and stalling the feed
findings.evidence: MorpheusDataFeed.UpdateFeedInternal asserts `timestamp >= existing.Timestamp` (contracts/MorpheusDataFeed/MorpheusDataFeed.cs:134) — strictly monotonic. The nitro-worker capability path (handleOracleFeed, reachable via capabilities.js:251 feed.publish/pricefeed) writes the SAME 'TWELVEDATA:<pair>' keys and uses the UPSTREAM provider timestamp unbounded: extractUpstreamTimestamp (feeds.js:209-238) parses TwelveData `datetime`/`timestamp`, then feeds.js:607-610 derives timestampSec = floor(Date.parse(quote.timestamp)/1000) with NO clamp to now, passed directly to updateFeed/updateFeeds (feed-submit.js:38-46,84). Meanwhile the production cron (feed-pusher.mjs planFeedUpdate:41) uses `ts = Math.max(cur.ts, now)` — always local-clock monotonic. If the worker publishes a record with a stale provider datetime, a later legit update with an older-but-still-valid provider time is rejected as 'stale timestamp'; worse, a provider returning a FUTURE datetime writes a far-future timestamp that permanently rejects every subsequent now-based push until a human runs AdminResetFeed.
findings.fix: Clamp the on-chain timestamp in the worker submission to a monotonic, bounded value: timestampSec = max(previousRecord.timestamp+1OrNow, min(Date.parse(quote.timestamp)/1000, now + SMALL_SKEW)). Reject (skip + log) any quote whose upstream timestamp is more than e.g. 5 min in the future or older than the last observed. Keep the human-readable observation time in the snapshot payload but submit a clamped value on-chain. Add a test in feeds.test.mjs for future-dated and stale-dated provider timestamps. Document that handleOracleFeed and feed-pusher.mjs must agree on the timestamp convention (both monotonic local-clock-bounded).
findings.risk: Clamping loses some fidelity of the true observation time on-chain; mitigated by keeping the raw upstream timestamp in the Supabase snapshot. The future-skew bound must be generous enough to tolerate normal NTP skew or it will drop valid updates.

findings.subsystem: nitro-worker-feeds (price-feed pipeline: workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/feeds/* + aggregation + deploy/feed-pusher)
findings.title: Feed staleness monitor only checks NEO-USD; ~35 other configured pairs can go stale or stuck with no alert
findings.evidence: deploy/feed-pusher/feed-monitor.mjs (runs every 15 min via morpheus-feed-monitor.timer) issues exactly one getLatest for 'TWELVEDATA:NEO-USD' (line 56-66) and only writes that one age + the updater GAS balance to feed-status.json. The pusher publishes the full SYMBOLS list (default NEO,GAS,BTC,ETH plus the registry defines ~35 pairs incl. forex/commodities/equities in feed-registry.js:9-179). A symbol that TwelveData silently stops serving (e.g., a delisted equity, a forex pair on a holiday) ages out with no monitor alert — feed-pusher's trackMissingSymbols only fires if the symbol was REQUESTED-and-missing for N cycles, and only the pusher log captures it, not the monitor/status surface. The monitor's NEO-USD-only view gives false 'ok'.
findings.fix: In feed-monitor.mjs, read getAllFeedRecords (already implemented in feed-pusher.mjs parseGetAllFeedRecordsStack) once, compute age per registered pair, and alert on any pair older than MAX_AGE; surface per-pair ages in feed-status.json. Keep the single-call batched read so monitor cost stays one RPC. Optionally cross-reference the configured SYMBOLS so a configured-but-never-published pair also alerts.
findings.risk: More alert volume; needs sensible per-category MAX_AGE (TradFi pairs legitimately don't update on weekends/holidays, so a flat-market staleness alert there is noise — exclude or widen the window for forex/commodity/equity pairs).

findings.subsystem: nitro-worker-feeds (price-feed pipeline: workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/feeds/* + aggregation + deploy/feed-pusher)
findings.title: providerResponseCache and providerBreakers Maps grow unbounded (no eviction)
findings.evidence: workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/providers.js: providerResponseCache (line 15) is keyed per distinct request (buildProviderCacheKey includes url+headers+pair, line 220-233) and entries are only ever set (line 645), never deleted on expiry — an expired entry is skipped on read (line 542) but stays resident. providerBreakers (line 19) accumulates one CircuitBreaker per provider id seen (getOrCreateBreaker, line 21) with no cap. providerConfigCache likewise only grows. In a long-lived enclave worker handling many distinct symbols/projects (each project_slug:provider:network is a distinct config key, line 289), these grow with cardinality of seen requests. Low absolute cost today (bounded by the static pair set on the feed path) but unbounded on the on-demand oracle.fetch path which accepts arbitrary symbols.
findings.fix: Add a cheap prune-on-set to providerResponseCache and providerConfigCache: when size exceeds a cap (e.g. 500), delete the oldest/expired entries (iterate and drop entries with expiresAt < now, or evict-oldest). The breaker map is naturally bounded by the small provider set, so leave it unless arbitrary provider ids can be introduced. No LRU subsystem needed.
findings.risk: Pruning a still-valid in-flight-related entry would just cause a refetch; harmless. Keep the cap well above the steady-state working set so the feed path never thrashes.

findings.subsystem: nitro-worker-feeds (price-feed pipeline: workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/feeds/* + aggregation + deploy/feed-pusher)
findings.title: feedStateCache holds an unbounded forever in-memory copy; a write failure silently desyncs disk from memory
findings.evidence: workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/feeds/feed-state.js: loadFeedState caches state per scoped path in feedStateCache (line 10,174) and saveFeedState updates the in-memory cache FIRST (line 181) then best-effort writes the file inside a try/catch that swallows all errors (line 182-192). If the disk write fails (full disk, permissions, EROFS in a constrained enclave), the in-memory cache reports the new state but disk has the old state — on the next process restart the worker silently reverts to the stale on-disk records and may re-submit/re-skip incorrectly (e.g. min-update-interval / threshold decisions computed against the wrong baseline). The failure is invisible: no log, no metric.
findings.fix: In saveFeedState, log (structured, level=error) and increment a failure counter when fs.writeFile throws, rather than swallowing silently; consider writing to a temp file + rename for atomicity so a partial write can't corrupt state. Surface persistent write failures to the worker's health/observability path so an operator knows feed state is non-durable.
findings.risk: Logging on every transient write error could be noisy if the underlying issue is chronic; gate behind a rate limiter or only log on transition (ok->failing).

findings.subsystem: nitro-worker-feeds (price-feed pipeline: workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/feeds/* + aggregation + deploy/feed-pusher)
findings.title: Two-source-divergent aggregation arbitrarily picks the lower price as 'used' with no provenance an on-chain consumer could act on
findings.evidence: workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/aggregation.js:61-74: with exactly 2 sources diverging beyond maxDeviationPct (default 25%), the code picks `prices[0] <= prices[1] ? 0 : 1` — i.e. always the LOWER price — and labels confidence 'low'. That is a guess (the lower number is not necessarily the correct one; a source returning 0 or a stale-low value would win). This is only reachable today via the HTTP fetchPriceQuotes response (not on-chain, since the on-chain path is single-source per finding #1), so impact is limited to API consumers, but if finding #1's aggregation-on-chain fix lands this becomes a price-correctness issue.
findings.fix: When 2 sources diverge beyond threshold, do not silently pick one: return confidence 'rejected'/no-consensus and let the caller decide (skip the publish). If a value must be returned for display, prefer the one closer to the last accepted price rather than the unconditional lower. Add a test asserting the divergent-2-source case is flagged, not silently resolved. Sequence this together with finding #1.
findings.risk: Returning no-consensus means 2-source pairs publish nothing on a divergence; acceptable for an oracle (fail-closed) but changes API response shape — coordinate with consumers.

findings.subsystem: nitro-worker-lanes (workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/* non-feed lanes: vrf, compute, decrypt/seal, neodid, http/oracle-query, message-reveal)
findings.title: NeoDID nullifier is derived from an UNVERIFIED, caller-supplied provider_uid for 9 of 10 providers
findings.evidence: workers/nitro-worker/src/neodid/index.js:181-213 resolveVerifiedProviderUid() only performs JWT/JWKS verification when provider==='web3auth'; for every other provider (twitter, github, google, discord, telegram, binance, okx, email, generic_oauth) it falls through to resolveProviderUid() (index.js:357-363) which returns trimString(payload.provider_uid || payload.social_uid || payload.user_id || ...) with NO verification. That UID feeds computeMasterNullifier/computeActionNullifier (index.js:239-259), which are the Sybil-resistance / identity-binding primitives for handleNeoDidBind, handleNeoDidActionTicket, handleNeoDidRecoveryTicket. The web bind route (apps/web/app/api/neodid/bind/route.ts:8-41) is a pure pass-through that forwards the raw body to the worker with no OAuth check, so the caller fully controls provider_uid. Yet handleNeoDidRuntime advertises `derives_provider_uid_in_tee: true` (index.js:523) unconditionally, which is only true for web3auth. Anyone able to reach the lane can mint a signed binding/recovery ticket for an arbitrary twitter/github/etc identity (and the recovery ticket signs a new_owner for an AA account, so this is account-takeover-adjacent if a contract trusts these nullifiers).
findings.fix: Either (a) require verified UID derivation for every supported provider (each must present a verifiable token the enclave checks, mirroring the web3auth path) and reject when verification material is absent, or (b) explicitly mark unverified providers as such in the SUPPORTED_PROVIDERS entry, gate them behind an env flag, and have handleNeoDidRuntime report `derives_provider_uid_in_tee` per-provider instead of a blanket true. At minimum, in resolveVerifiedProviderUid throw for non-web3auth providers unless an explicit MORPHEUS_NEODID_ALLOW_UNVERIFIED_UID opt-in is set, so the default posture is fail-safe.
findings.risk: If contracts already consume these nullifiers in production, tightening verification is a breaking change for the 9 unverified providers; needs a coordinated rollout. If left as-is, recovery/zklogin tickets are forgeable for any unverified identity.

findings.subsystem: nitro-worker-lanes (workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/* non-feed lanes: vrf, compute, decrypt/seal, neodid, http/oracle-query, message-reveal)
findings.title: /oracle/decrypt is a TEE decryption oracle with no per-request gating — security rests entirely on the shared worker token
findings.evidence: workers/nitro-worker/src/capabilities.js:58-75 handleOracleDecrypt() decrypts ANY X25519 envelope sealed to the oracle key and returns the plaintext, with zero binding to a requester, message id, unlock time, or on-chain state. The reassuring comment (capabilities.js:53-57) says the time-lock is enforced upstream — and indeed the relayer (workers/morpheus-relayer/src/fulfillment.js:680-682) only calls it after the kernel gated `confidential.decrypt`. But the worker lane itself enforces nothing: any holder of the worker API token (requireAuth, platform/auth.js:11-28) can decrypt any captured ciphertext for any Neo Message, defeating the time-lock entirely. Contrast with the sibling message-reveal lane (oracle/message-reveal.js) which binds an EIP-191 signature to chain+contract+messageId+freshness and reads the message from a trusted contract before decrypting. /oracle/decrypt has none of that defense-in-depth.
findings.fix: Add request-context binding to /oracle/decrypt comparable to message-reveal: accept (chain, contract, messageId), read the message + unlockTime from the worker-configured trusted contract, and refuse to decrypt unless block.timestamp >= unlockTime (the same check the contract supposedly already did, re-asserted in the enclave). This makes the lane fail-safe even if the worker token leaks or the relayer is tricked into calling it early.
findings.risk: Requires the relayer to pass message context on the confidential.decrypt path; behavior-preserving for legitimate callers but adds a chain read to the decrypt hot path.

findings.subsystem: nitro-worker-lanes (workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/* non-feed lanes: vrf, compute, decrypt/seal, neodid, http/oracle-query, message-reveal)
findings.title: oracle-query / smart-fetch surface upstream/transient failures as HTTP 400, hiding the real cause from callers and from idempotency caching
findings.evidence: workers/nitro-worker/src/capabilities.js:99-105 handleOracleQuery/handleOracleSmartFetch call `json(200, await buildOracleResponse(...))` with no try/catch. buildOracleResponse (oracle/fetch.js:344-408) -> performOracleFetch throws on a non-ok upstream (`throw new Error(buildUpstreamErrorMessage(url, response.status, ...))` at fetch.js:328) and on fetch timeout (fetch.js:210-213). Those throws propagate to the worker-level catch (worker.js:200-216) which maps EVERYTHING to `json(400, {error})`. So an upstream 503/timeout/circuit-breaker-open becomes a 400 to the caller — indistinguishable from a malformed request. request-guards.js:330-339 deliberately only caches 2xx + a small 4xx set, so this also means transient upstream blips are (correctly) not cached, but the 400 status misleads clients into not retrying.
findings.fix: Wrap the oracle-query/smart-fetch handlers (and ideally classify in buildOracleResponse) so upstream/transport failures return 502/504 and only genuine input validation returns 400. Tag the error with a machine-readable `kind` (e.g. upstream_error|timeout|breaker_open|bad_request). This restores retry semantics and aligns with the message-reveal lane which already returns 502 on chain-read failure (message-reveal.js:143-147).
findings.risk: Changing status codes could affect any caller that currently treats all errors as 4xx; low if the edge/relayer just logs the body.

findings.subsystem: nitro-worker-lanes (workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/* non-feed lanes: vrf, compute, decrypt/seal, neodid, http/oracle-query, message-reveal)
findings.title: Lanes return inconsistent success/error envelope shapes — no single canonical result contract across vrf/compute/oracle-query/neodid
findings.evidence: Each lane hand-rolls its top-level shape. VRF (oracle/vrf.js:10-20) returns {randomness, signature, public_key, attestation_hash, tee_attestation, verification, ...} but OMITS `output_hash` that every other signed lane includes. compute (compute/index.js:713-728) returns {mode, target_chain, ...result, output_hash, signature, public_key, attestation_hash, tee_attestation, verification}. oracle-query (fetch.js:370-389) returns yet another shape with body/body_json/status_code/extracted_value. neodid (neodid/index.js:475-488) spreads result + {mode, output_hash, attestation_hash, verification, tee_attestation} but NOT top-level signature/public_key (those live only inside verification). Error shapes also diverge: handleOracleDecrypt/handleMessageReveal/handleComputeExecute use jsonError (sanitized) while handleVrf/handleOracleQuery/neodid handlers have no try/catch and rely on the worker-level catch — meaning their error body is `{error}` only when no executionPlan, but a full result-envelope when one exists (worker.js:202-213). A consumer cannot write one verification routine.
findings.fix: Define a shared success-envelope builder in chain/signing.js (or packages/shared) that every signed lane spreads identically — at minimum {output_hash, attestation_hash, signature, public_key, verification, tee_attestation} — and have VRF use it (it currently drops output_hash). Route all lane error returns through jsonError so the error body shape is uniform regardless of executionPlan. Add a versioned `envelope_version` field.
findings.risk: Adding output_hash to VRF and standardizing fields is additive (safe); reordering/removing fields could break consumers that read positionally — keep all existing keys, only add.

findings.subsystem: nitro-worker-lanes (workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/* non-feed lanes: vrf, compute, decrypt/seal, neodid, http/oracle-query, message-reveal)
findings.title: VRF (and /oracle/decrypt) have no built-in replay/idempotency dedup; default deploy with Upstash guards disabled returns fresh randomness per call with no request_id binding
findings.evidence: handleVrf (oracle/vrf.js:5-21) generates crypto.getRandomValues each call and signs it; if payload.request_id is absent it fabricates one (`payload.request_id || crypto.randomUUID()`) so the randomness is NOT bound to a stable request id. The only dedup is request-guards.js routePolicy vrf_random idempotency, which (a) is entirely off unless MORPHEUS_UPSTASH_GUARDS_ENABLED and isUpstashEnabled() (request-guards.js:85-86,256), and (b) keys idempotency on operationHash derived from request_id (request-guards.js:159-167,186-187) — absent a request_id there is none. The relayer's own VRF path (fulfillment.js:654-674) is the production money path and is fine, but the HTTP /vrf/random lane, if reachable, will hand a different signed randomness to two retries of the same logical request. The signing-layer seenRequestIds dedup (chain/signing.js:83-103) is never consulted by handleVrf. There is also no vrf.test.mjs (confirmed: no vrf test file exists).
findings.fix: In handleVrf, require a request_id (reject if missing for non-interactive callers) and bind the signed payload to it; consult rememberRequestId(request_id) from chain/signing.js so a duplicate request_id returns the same cached randomness (or is rejected) even without Upstash. Add a vrf.test.mjs covering: deterministic-per-request_id behavior, signature verifies, and missing-key fail path.
findings.risk: If any current caller relies on calling /vrf/random without a request_id, requiring one is breaking — make it opt-in via a flag or only enforce dedup when request_id is supplied.

findings.subsystem: nitro-worker-lanes (workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/* non-feed lanes: vrf, compute, decrypt/seal, neodid, http/oracle-query, message-reveal)
findings.title: privacy.add_noise can return -Infinity/NaN (Laplace sampler divides by log(0)) and the catalog oversells it as differential privacy
findings.evidence: compute/index.js:113-121: cryptoRandomUnit() = randomBytes(6).readUIntBE(0,6)/0x1000000000000 yields [0,1). addLaplaceNoise computes u = cryptoRandomUnit()-0.5 in [-0.5, ~0.5) then noise = -scale*sign(u)*log(1-2*|u|). When the 48-bit draw is exactly 0, u = -0.5, |u| = 0.5, log(1-1) = log(0) = -Infinity, so noisy_value is -Infinity (and Math.sign(0) handling for u===0 gives noise 0, losing the negative tail). The BUILTIN_COMPUTE_CATALOG entry (compute/index.js:467-471) describes it as 'simulated Laplace noise for differential privacy' which implies a privacy guarantee this naive sampler does not provide (no sensitivity/epsilon accounting).
findings.fix: Clamp the uniform draw into (0,1) exclusive (e.g. (draw+0.5)/2^48) before the inverse-CDF so 1-2|u| is never 0; or use the standard u1-u2 of two exponentials. Re-label the catalog description as 'adds Laplace noise (illustrative; not a calibrated DP mechanism)' or take an explicit epsilon/sensitivity and document the guarantee.
findings.risk: None; it is a planning/demo builtin, but a NaN result silently corrupts any caller that on-chain-consumes it.

findings.subsystem: nitro-worker-lanes (workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/* non-feed lanes: vrf, compute, decrypt/seal, neodid, http/oracle-query, message-reveal)
findings.title: Pre-auth /attestation and /info endpoints accept and hash arbitrary caller-controlled report_data with no size/shape limit
findings.evidence: worker.js:136-153 handles /health, /info, and /attestation BEFORE requireAuth (worker.js:155). /attestation takes payload.report_data|reportData|output_hash|message (any caller-supplied string/object) and passes it to buildDstackAttestation -> normalizeReportData (nitro-signer.js:218-227) which sha256's stableStringify of arbitrary input, then makes an unauthenticated outbound POST to the enclave /attest endpoint (nitro-signer.js:231-253). Because it is pre-auth and the body is parsed via request.json() with no overload-guard (acquireOverloadSlot runs only after auth at worker.js:160), an unauthenticated client can drive enclave attestation round-trips and large-JSON hashing at will (the 10s AbortSignal bounds each but not concurrency).
findings.fix: Move /attestation behind requireAuth (it returns enclave attestation material — there is no clear reason it must be public), or at minimum cap report_data length and run it through the overload guard / a dedicated low rate limit before the enclave call.
findings.risk: If a monitoring probe currently fetches /attestation unauthenticated, gating it breaks that probe; verify before requiring auth.

findings.subsystem: packages-shared (@neo-morpheus-oracle/shared): types, utils, result-envelope, workflow-catalog, public-runtime, rate-limit, confidential-envelope, node-runtime)
findings.title: Canonical confidential-envelope crypto is not exported and is silently duplicated in apps/web with no drift guard
findings.evidence: packages/shared/src/confidential-envelope.js is the documented 'single reference implementation' of the X25519-HKDF-SHA256-AES-256-GCM envelope (header lines 1-37), yet it is NOT in package.json exports (only ., ./utils, ./rate-limit, ./public-runtime, ./node-runtime are mapped) and has ZERO in-repo importers (grep finds only its own test). apps/web/lib/browser-encryption.ts is a hand-vendored re-implementation of the exact same envelope (same ENVELOPE_VERSION/ALGORITHM/INFO, same deriveAesKey info-concat order and salt) that does not import the canonical module and cannot (it's unexported). The header claims a 'sync_morpheus_registry drift guard' keeps copies aligned, but grep for sync_morpheus_registry / morpheus-confidential-envelope finds NO such guard in this repo. The two implementations are byte-aligned today but nothing enforces it; a one-byte drift in key-derivation breaks decryption of live confidential client traffic (the module's own comment says exactly this).
findings.fix: Add "./confidential-envelope": "./src/confidential-envelope.js" to packages/shared/package.json exports (with a confidential-envelope.d.ts), then have apps/web/lib/browser-encryption.ts import encryptConfidentialEnvelope from @neo-morpheus-oracle/shared/confidential-envelope and delete the vendored crypto. If vendoring must remain for bundle reasons, add an in-repo parity test (the golden vector already exists at confidential-envelope.test.mjs:33) that runs browser-encryption.ts against the canonical golden vector so drift is caught in CI. Also correct/remove the header reference to the non-existent sync_morpheus_registry guard.
findings.risk: Low for the exports addition (additive). Medium if deleting the vendored copy: browser-encryption.ts uses window.crypto/window.atob and runs in the browser bundle — verify the shared module's globalThis.crypto fallbacks bundle cleanly under the web app's bundler before deleting.

findings.subsystem: packages-shared (@neo-morpheus-oracle/shared): types, utils, result-envelope, workflow-catalog, public-runtime, rate-limit, confidential-envelope, node-runtime)
findings.title: getPublicRuntimeStatusNotes throws on snapshots whose catalog.automation lacks triggerKinds (builder doesn't guarantee the field the reader requires)
findings.evidence: public-runtime.js:140 does `snapshot.catalog.automation.triggerKinds.join(', ')`, but buildPublicRuntimeCatalogSummary (public-runtime.js:95) sets `automation: clone(catalog?.automation || {})` — i.e. it does NOT guarantee automation.triggerKinds is an array. The web status page calls getPublicRuntimeStatusNotes on a snapshot parsed from an arbitrary network response (apps/web/app/status/page.tsx:124) gated only by isRuntimeStatusSnapshot (page.tsx:76-81) which checks just `'runtime' in value && 'catalog' in value` — it does NOT validate catalog.automation.triggerKinds. A degraded/emergency runtime, an older snapshot schema, or any 200 response with {runtime,catalog} but empty automation makes .triggerKinds undefined → TypeError 'Cannot read properties of undefined (reading join)'.
findings.fix: In getPublicRuntimeStatusNotes guard the access: `const triggerKinds = Array.isArray(snapshot.catalog?.automation?.triggerKinds) ? snapshot.catalog.automation.triggerKinds : []` and only push the Automation note when non-empty; likewise default executionPlane/riskPlane reads. Optionally have buildPublicRuntimeCatalogSummary normalize automation to `{ ...clone(automation), triggerKinds: Array.isArray(automation?.triggerKinds) ? [...] : [] }` so the contract is self-consistent.
findings.risk: Very low; pure defensiveness. The current crash is caught by the surrounding try/catch on the status page (it merely mislabels a service as failing), so this is hardening, not an outage.

findings.subsystem: packages-shared (@neo-morpheus-oracle/shared): types, utils, result-envelope, workflow-catalog, public-runtime, rate-limit, confidential-envelope, node-runtime)
findings.title: stableStringify (signing-critical) sorts keys with locale-default localeCompare, an environment-dependent collation
findings.evidence: utils.js:120 sorts object keys with `a.localeCompare(b)` — no locale and no Intl.Collator. String.prototype.localeCompare without an explicit locale uses the host's default locale + ICU collation, which can vary across Node/ICU versions, OS locale, and the LANG/LC_* env. This function is the canonical input to the worker's signed output_hash (stable-stringify-vectors.mjs:1-7: 'chain/signing.js hashes stableStringify(result) before signing ... a single byte of drift breaks output-hash verification against live signatures'). The relayer, nitro-worker (enclave), and web verifier are distinct runtimes; if any has a different default collation for mixed-case/unicode keys (e.g. the {gamma,Beta,alpha} and unicode-key vectors at lines 53-79), the produced ordering diverges and verification fails. The golden-vector test (stable-stringify.test.mjs) only proves correctness on the CI machine, not cross-runtime.
findings.fix: Pin the collation deterministically without changing today's output: replace the bare localeCompare with a fixed collator, e.g. `const collator = new Intl.Collator('en', { sensitivity: 'variant', caseFirst: 'false' })` (or whatever reproduces the existing golden vectors) and sort with `collator.compare(a, b)`. Verify ALL STABLE_STRINGIFY_GOLDEN_VECTORS still pass byte-for-byte after the change — the pinned collator must reproduce the current bytes, not 'fix' the ordering. This removes the dependence on host default locale.
findings.risk: Medium — this is the live signing path. The change must be byte-identical to current output (gated by the golden vectors). Do NOT switch to code-unit ordering (would break live signatures, explicitly forbidden in the vector header).

findings.subsystem: packages-shared (@neo-morpheus-oracle/shared): types, utils, result-envelope, workflow-catalog, public-runtime, rate-limit, confidential-envelope, node-runtime)
findings.title: mapWithConcurrency exists in shared but is still copy-pasted in 3 relayer modules, two with NON-fail-fast semantics
findings.evidence: The fail-fast bounded-concurrency helper is exported from utils.js:74 (and typed in utils.d.ts:11) and consumed correctly by neo-n3.js:4. But three relayer modules redefine it locally despite already importing other helpers from @neo-morpheus-oracle/shared/utils: neox.js:185 (identical fail-fast copy — pure duplication), request-processor.js:352 and queue.js:525 (both `while(true)` with NO `failed` guard — i.e. NON-fail-fast: a thrown worker does not freeze the cursor, other workers keep pulling/issuing RPC for the rest of the tick). So the shared 'one definition, one test' contract is undermined by 3 divergent in-tree copies, and the two queue/request-processor copies have materially different abort semantics than the documented shared version.
findings.fix: Delete neox.js:185-207 and import mapWithConcurrency from @neo-morpheus-oracle/shared/utils (drop-in, identical). For request-processor.js:352 and queue.js:525, decide intent per call-site: if a faulted scan/tick must abort (cursor safety), switch to the shared fail-fast import and delete the local copy; if 'attempt every item regardless' is genuinely required, keep a clearly-named local mapWithConcurrencySettleAll and document why it differs (the shared helper's own doc-comment at utils.js:62-72 already warns callers needing all-attempted must NOT use it). Either way, eliminate the verbatim duplicates.
findings.risk: Low for neox.js (identical). Medium for queue/request-processor: changing to fail-fast alters tick behavior on partial failure — verify against queue/request-processor tests and the cursor-advance-on-success invariant before swapping.

findings.subsystem: packages-shared (@neo-morpheus-oracle/shared): types, utils, result-envelope, workflow-catalog, public-runtime, rate-limit, confidential-envelope, node-runtime)
findings.title: Public-runtime contract types are defined twice (index.ts vs public-runtime.d.ts) with no single source
findings.evidence: index.ts:193-236 declares RuntimeProbeSnapshotInput, PublicRuntimeCatalogSummary, and PublicRuntimeStatusSnapshot inline; public-runtime.d.ts:1-44 declares the same three types again. index.ts re-exports the public-runtime VALUES (lines 238-243) but redeclares the TYPES rather than re-exporting them. The two definitions are identical today, so a change to one (e.g. adding a runtime.status variant or an info field) silently desyncs the bare-root type surface from the ./public-runtime subpath surface. Consumers split across both: OverviewTab.tsx:10 imports from /public-runtime, others would get index.ts's copy.
findings.fix: Make index.ts re-export the types instead of redeclaring them: `export type { RuntimeProbeSnapshotInput, PublicRuntimeCatalogSummary, PublicRuntimeStatusSnapshot } from './public-runtime.d.ts'` (mirroring how it already re-exports UpstashRateLimit types from ./rate-limit), and delete the inline declarations at index.ts:193-236. One source of truth for the public-runtime contract.
findings.risk: Very low; type-only change, no runtime impact. Verify the bare-root type import path resolves under the consumers' moduleResolution (bundler).

findings.subsystem: packages-shared (@neo-morpheus-oracle/shared): types, utils, result-envelope, workflow-catalog, public-runtime, rate-limit, confidential-envelope, node-runtime)
findings.title: public-runtime.js redefines trimString with DIFFERENT semantics than the package's own canonical utils.trimString
findings.evidence: public-runtime.js:1 defines `trimString = typeof value === 'string' ? value.trim() : ''` while the canonical export utils.js:14 is `String(value || '').trim()`. These differ for non-strings: utils coerces (trimString(123) -> '123', trimString(true) -> 'true') whereas the public-runtime copy returns ''. public-runtime.js consumes only string-ish probe fields today so it works, but having two same-named helpers with divergent behavior inside ONE package is a trap for future edits (a refactor that routes a numeric field through the local copy would silently change behavior). confidential-envelope.js:62 also redefines trimString but is justified (documented dependency-free/vendorable); public-runtime.js has no such justification.
findings.fix: Have public-runtime.js import trimString (and isPlainObject if a shared one is introduced) from ./utils.js instead of redefining it, OR rename the local one to something intent-revealing (e.g. trimStringStrict) with a comment that it intentionally does not coerce. Both eliminate the same-name/different-behavior hazard inside the package.
findings.risk: Very low. If switching to utils.trimString, confirm no public-runtime path relies on the non-coercing '' return for non-strings (the readString wrapper already only feeds it probe body fields).

findings.subsystem: packages-shared (@neo-morpheus-oracle/shared): types, utils, result-envelope, workflow-catalog, public-runtime, rate-limit, confidential-envelope, node-runtime)
findings.title: Subpath modules rate-limit and node-runtime have no .d.ts; index.ts type-re-exports from rate-limit.js are latent TS resolution failures
findings.evidence: package.json maps ./rate-limit and ./node-runtime to plain .js with no `types` condition, and src/ has no rate-limit.d.ts or node-runtime.d.ts (only utils.d.ts and public-runtime.d.ts exist). index.ts:186 does `export type { UpstashRateLimitConfig, UpstashRateLimitResult } from './rate-limit.js'` — but rate-limit.js exports no such types, so this re-export resolves to nothing/any. Today no .ts/.tsx file imports the bare-root @neo-morpheus-oracle/shared (only .js workers do) and no TS file imports the rate-limit types, so it's dormant; but any future TS consumer of applyUpstashRateLimit's typed config or of /node-runtime (env/sha256Hex/ensureBuffer) gets implicit-any / TS7016, with skipLibCheck:true masking it.
findings.fix: Add rate-limit.d.ts (declaring applyUpstashRateLimit plus the UpstashRateLimitConfig/UpstashRateLimitResult types that index.ts already tries to re-export) and node-runtime.d.ts (env/ensureBuffer/sha256Hex), and add `types` conditions to those two exports entries in package.json. Confirm the index.ts:186 type re-export then resolves to a real declaration.
findings.risk: Very low; additive .d.ts files plus exports `types` keys. No runtime change.

findings.subsystem: edge-control-plane (deploy/cloudflare/morpheus-edge-gateway + morpheus-control-plane)
findings.title: Dead-lettered jobs are never marked terminal; cron recovery re-requeues permanently-failing jobs forever (infinite loop)
findings.evidence: deploy/cloudflare/morpheus-control-plane/worker.mjs:249-261 `queue(batch, env)` only handles batch.queue === 'morpheus-oracle-request' and 'morpheus-feed-tick'; there is NO consumer bound to the configured DLQs `morpheus-oracle-request-dlq`/`morpheus-feed-tick-dlq` (wrangler.meshmini.toml:33,40). When a message exhausts max_retries=10 and Cloudflare routes it to the DLQ, the Supabase row stays non-terminal (queued/processing/dispatched). `dead_lettered` appears only in the TERMINAL_JOB_STATUSES set (queue-consumer.js:11) and is NEVER assigned anywhere in the codebase. The `*/5 * * * *` cron (worker.mjs:268 -> recoverNetworkJobs) calls listRecoverableJobs (recovery.js:8-44) which re-lists these non-terminal rows and requeueJob (recovery.js:54-93) re-enqueues a brand-new message + resets status to 'dispatched' with NO requeue-count ceiling. That new message will also fail 10x, dead-letter again, and be re-requeued at the next cron tick — indefinitely. README claims DLQ makes loss 'inspectable instead of silent' but the row is neither resolved nor stopped.
findings.fix: Add a DLQ consumer: bind `[[queues.consumers]] queue="morpheus-oracle-request-dlq"` (+ feed-tick-dlq) in both wrangler tomls and route them in worker.mjs `queue()` to a handler that patchJob(status:'dead_lettered', completed_at, error) for the job_id in the message body, so the row becomes terminal and the cron stops re-listing it. Alternatively (or additionally) add a requeue ceiling in recovery.js: track metadata.requeue_attempts, and once it exceeds a max (e.g. 3) patchJob to 'dead_lettered' instead of re-enqueuing. Without one of these, a poison job burns queue+execution-plane resources on every 5-min tick.
findings.risk: Behavior change to recovery path; gate with a unit test asserting a DLQ message marks the row dead_lettered and the cron no longer re-lists it. Low blast radius (new consumer + one status transition).

findings.subsystem: edge-control-plane (deploy/cloudflare/morpheus-edge-gateway + morpheus-control-plane)
findings.title: Rate-limit backend failure throws an unhandled exception (generic 500/1101) instead of the intended fail-closed 503
findings.evidence: packages/shared/src/rate-limit.js `applyUpstashRateLimit` THROWS `upstash pipeline failed (status)` when Upstash returns non-200 (it only returns null when Upstash is UNCONFIGURED). In the edge gateway, applyUpstashRateLimitImpl (worker.mjs:212-232) calls it without try/catch, and applyNativeRateLimit is invoked at worker.mjs:437 with no surrounding try/catch (the only try in fetch wraps the origin fetch at :453). Same in control-plane auth.js:44-63 applyRateLimit -> no try/catch, and worker.mjs:140 `applyRateLimit` is outside any try (the try starts at :192 around insertJob). So when Upstash is reachable but erroring (5xx/4xx/network blip mid-request), the worker throws an uncaught exception → Cloudflare returns an opaque 500/1101, NOT the deliberate `503 rate_limit_backend_unavailable` the code returns on the `result===null`-but-configured path. The fail-closed design intent (worker.mjs:231, auth.js:62) is bypassed by the throw.
findings.fix: Wrap the `applyUpstashRateLimit` call in applyUpstashRateLimitImpl (edge) and applyRateLimit (control-plane) in try/catch; on throw, return the same `json(503,{error:'rate_limit_backend_unavailable'})` as the unconfigured-error path so the failure mode is the documented fail-closed 503 with a clean body, not an opaque worker crash. Add a test (currently none — gateway test only covers origin-hang 503, control-plane test has zero rate-limit-backend assertions).
findings.risk: Pure error-path hardening; no change to the success or limit-exceeded paths.

findings.subsystem: edge-control-plane (deploy/cloudflare/morpheus-edge-gateway + morpheus-control-plane)
findings.title: No CORS headers on the tokenless public GETs — oracle consoles cannot do live browser fetches; a scoped allow-list is warranted and safe
findings.evidence: Neither edge worker emits any Access-Control-Allow-* header (grep confirms zero CORS in deploy/cloudflare/). The web app sets CORS on exactly one route (apps/web/app/api/neodid/resolve/route.ts:18 `access-control-allow-origin:*`) and nowhere else. The gateway already classifies a set of safe, idempotent, tokenless GETs (CACHE_RULES worker.mjs:6-21: /health, /providers, /feeds/catalog, /feeds/price/*, /oracle/public-key) — exactly the data the 60 oracle consoles want to render. A browser GET from a console origin to oracle.meshmini.app today is blocked by the same-origin policy because no ACAO header is returned, forcing every console through a server-side proxy. There is also no OPTIONS preflight handler (grep: no OPTIONS in either worker), so any non-simple cross-origin request fails preflight outright.
findings.fix: In the edge gateway add a CORS layer scoped to the GET-cacheable routes only: for requests matching a CACHE_RULE (read-only, tokenless), set `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` (echo a configured allow-list from env e.g. MORPHEUS_EDGE_CORS_ORIGINS, or '*' for these purely-public reads since they carry no credentials/secrets), `Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, OPTIONS`, `Vary: Origin`, and handle a short-circuit OPTIONS 204 preflight BEFORE auth/turnstile/rate-limit. Critically, do NOT add CORS to the RUNTIME_AUTH_REQUIRED_PATHS or TURNSTILE_PROTECTED_PATHS (worker.mjs:23-38) — those are credentialed/abuse-prone and must stay non-CORS. This is fully code-fixable (no infra/cred blocker); '*' on tokenless public reads is safe because they expose no per-user/secret data and bear no cookies.
findings.risk: Must scope the allow strictly to the read-only tokenless route set; mistakenly allowing CORS on POST/auth routes would be a real exposure. Add a test asserting CORS headers appear on /feeds/price and are ABSENT on /paymaster/authorize.

findings.subsystem: edge-control-plane (deploy/cloudflare/morpheus-edge-gateway + morpheus-control-plane)
findings.title: Turnstile body-token path can consume the request body before it is forwarded to origin (prefixed POST routes)
findings.evidence: For a network-prefixed POST (e.g. /testnet/paymaster/authorize), worker.mjs:411-414 builds `turnstileRequest = new Request(url, request)` from the original request (transferring its body), then verifyTurnstile (worker.mjs:150-159) does `const cloned = request.clone(); ... await cloned.json()` to read the turnstile token from the JSON body when no header token is present. After turnstile passes, buildOriginRequest(request, env) (worker.mjs:447 -> :252-258) forwards the ORIGINAL `request.body`. Constructing `new Request(input, request)` ties/consumes the original request's body stream; combined with the clone+json read, the original request.body can be disturbed/locked by the time it is forwarded, breaking the proxied POST body to origin. This fires ONLY when (a) a Turnstile secret is configured AND (b) the client puts the token in the JSON body rather than the cf-turnstile-token/x-turnstile-token header (worker.mjs:152-159 reads the header first), so it is a narrow path — but it silently corrupts paymaster/relay/compute/vrf POST bodies when it hits.
findings.fix: Read the body once: in verifyTurnstile, when a body read is needed, read text from the clone and parse, and have the caller reuse that already-read body for the origin request (pass the buffered body through buildOriginRequest) rather than relying on the original request.body stream after it may have been consumed. Or require the turnstile token via header only on prefixed routes. Add a test for a prefixed turnstile-protected POST with the token in the JSON body asserting the origin still receives the full body.
findings.risk: Touches the request-forwarding path for credentialed POSTs; verify against the existing oracle-feed POST test (worker.test.mjs:181) and add the body-token case.
