findings.subsystem: sdk-integration
findings.title: Canonical encryption primitive (encryptConfidentialEnvelope) is not exported from @neo-morpheus-oracle/shared, forcing every integrator to copy it
findings.evidence: packages/shared/src/confidential-envelope.js:1-37 documents itself as 'the single reference implementation' of the X25519-HKDF-SHA256-AES-256-GCM envelope and is explicitly written to be 'dependency-free so it can be vendored verbatim'. But packages/shared/package.json:6-14 exports map has NO entry for it, and packages/shared/src/index.ts/index.js do not re-export encryptConfidentialEnvelope/encryptConfidentialEnvelope. Consequence: external consumers cannot `import { encryptConfidentialEnvelope } from '@neo-morpheus-oracle/shared/...'`. Instead every consumer hand-maintains a copy guarded by a drift checker (lines 28-32 list apps/web/lib/browser-encryption.ts, examples/*, neo-miniapps-platform, neo-abstract-account as 'known downstream copies'). examples/scripts/common.mjs:430-492 and examples/browser-encryption/encrypt-oracle-payload.js:25-87 are two such copies.
findings.fix: Add `"./confidential-envelope": "./src/confidential-envelope.js"` to packages/shared/package.json exports, and re-export encryptConfidentialEnvelope/decryptConfidentialEnvelope/parseConfidentialEnvelope/CONFIDENTIAL_ENVELOPE_* from packages/shared/src/index.ts (+ index.d.ts types). Then replace the hand-rolled copies in common.mjs and examples/browser-encryption with imports, collapsing the encrypt logic to one source. This makes the documented 'single reference implementation' actually consumable and removes ~3 in-repo crypto copies.
findings.risk: Crypto wire format must stay byte-identical; confidential-envelope.test.mjs already pins it via golden vectors, so adding an export does not change behavior. The in-repo copy replacement is verified by the existing drift guard + envelope tests.

findings.subsystem: sdk-integration
findings.title: Quickstart and oracle docs reference a function name (encryptWithOracleX25519) that exists nowhere in the codebase
findings.evidence: apps/web/app/docs/quickstart/page.tsx:113 and apps/web/app/docs/oracle/page.tsx:205 both show `await encryptWithOracleX25519(JSON.stringify(secrets), public_key)`. grep across the repo (excluding node_modules/.next) finds zero definitions of `encryptWithOracleX25519` — the actual provided helper is named `encryptWithOracleKey` (examples/browser-encryption/encrypt-oracle-payload.js:25, examples/scripts/common.mjs:430) and the canonical one is `encryptConfidentialEnvelope`. A developer copy-pasting the quickstart gets a ReferenceError.
findings.fix: Either rename the docs snippet to the real, importable function name (after exporting encryptConfidentialEnvelope per the prior finding, reference that), or add an `encryptWithOracleX25519` named alias/wrapper that is actually shipped (e.g. in examples/browser-encryption and exported from shared). Make the docs snippet self-contained or link directly to the runnable example file.
findings.risk: Docs-only change; low risk. Should pick one canonical name and use it consistently across quickstart, oracle, and the example file.

findings.subsystem: sdk-integration
findings.title: Example encryption helpers silently encrypt to a null key when the oracle public-key endpoint is degraded (200 + public_key:null)
findings.evidence: apps/web/app/api/oracle/public-key/route.ts:45-60 returns HTTP 200 with {available:false, degraded:true, public_key:null} whenever upstream is 401/403/408/429/5xx (shouldDegradePublicKeyStatus). The browser example examples/browser-encryption/encrypt-oracle-payload.js:1-7 only checks `response.ok` (true for the degraded 200) then encryptSecretOnly/encryptConfidentialJsonPatch (lines 89-97) call encryptWithOracleKey(oracleKey.public_key=null,...) -> decodeBase64ToBytes(null) -> atob(null) yields a bogus key. The quickstart pattern (quickstart/page.tsx:104-113) reproduces this: `const { public_key } = await res.json()` with no availability check. The result is an encrypted blob bound to a key nobody holds, submitted on-chain (fee paid), undecryptable. This is the documented happy path and the oracle has real degraded periods.
findings.fix: In both example helpers (and the quickstart snippet) check `if (!body.available || !body.public_key) throw new Error('oracle public key unavailable: ' + (body.message||body.error))` before encrypting; do not rely on response.ok for the proxied /api/oracle/public-key path. Optionally have the proxy still return the degraded body but with a non-200 status for the encrypt-intent caller, or document the available flag in quickstart.
findings.risk: Example/docs change; the node-encryption example hits the Nitro runtime directly (native shape) so it already throws on non-ok — only the browser/proxy path is affected.

findings.subsystem: sdk-integration
findings.title: Core request/poll integration helpers are copy-pasted across 16-18 example scripts instead of being exported from common.mjs
findings.evidence: The request->poll->verify loop logic — parseStackItem (18 files), decodeCallbackArray (15 files), waitForRequestId (16 files), waitForCallback (15 files) — is redefined verbatim in nearly every examples/scripts/test-n3-*.mjs (e.g. test-n3-examples.mjs:78-117, test-n3-aa-paymaster-automation-oracle.mjs:923-957). examples/scripts/common.mjs exports 30+ helpers but NOT these — the single most reused integration primitives. A fix to the requestId-extraction logic (e.g. a new event name) must be applied 16 times.
findings.fix: Move parseStackItem, decodeCallbackArray, waitForRequestId, waitForCallback into examples/scripts/common.mjs (exported) and replace the per-file copies with imports. These are the canonical 'how to get your requestId and read your callback' helpers; centralizing them also makes them documentable as the reference polling pattern.
findings.risk: Behavior-preserving extraction; the live example test suite (examples:test:n3 etc.) exercises all of them, so regressions surface immediately. Watch for the few scripts whose waitForRequestId uses a different event-name set.

findings.subsystem: sdk-integration
findings.title: Quickstart tells integrators to 'get the requestId from your transaction' but never explains it comes from the OracleRequested/MiniAppRequestQueued notification (the #1 integration gotcha)
findings.evidence: apps/web/app/docs/quickstart/page.tsx:258 ('Check your transaction to get the requestId') gives no mechanism. The actual requestId is not readable from request()'s return value when invoked via a wallet (wallet relay returns only {txid}, no post-exec stack); it must be parsed from the application-log notification, exactly as examples/scripts/test-n3-examples.mjs:78-93 waitForRequestId does (getApplicationLog -> find OracleRequested/MiniAppRequestQueued -> state.value[0]). No docs page surfaces this, so integrators hit the dead end of expecting a synchronous BigInteger return that wallets don't deliver.
findings.fix: Add a short 'Getting your requestId' block to quickstart/oracle docs showing getapplicationlog notification extraction (reference the now-exported waitForRequestId helper). Note explicitly that the request() BigInteger return is only available in contract-to-contract calls, not via wallet relay.
findings.risk: Docs addition; no code risk. High clarity payoff given this is the most reported friction point per project history.

findings.subsystem: sdk-integration
findings.title: The public HTTP oracle API (/api/oracle/query, /api/oracle/smart-fetch, /api/compute/execute) is undocumented in the API Reference
findings.evidence: apps/web/app/api/oracle/query/route.ts and smart-fetch/route.ts expose rate-limited (30/min, 20/min) public POST endpoints that return results synchronously — a distinct integration model from the on-chain async callback. But apps/web/app/docs/api-reference/page.tsx only documents the Neo N3 Contract.Call pattern and NeoDID resolve; grep finds zero docs pages referencing /api/oracle/query or smart-fetch. Integrators have no documented request schema (OraclePayload shape lives only in packages/shared/src/index.ts:75-95), no rate-limit/error contract, and no guidance on when to use HTTP vs on-chain.
findings.fix: Add an HTTP API section to the api-reference docs (or a new docs page): request/response schema for /api/oracle/query and /api/oracle/smart-fetch (derive from OraclePayload), rate limits, error_code contract, and a 'HTTP synchronous vs on-chain async' decision note. Link from quickstart.
findings.risk: Docs-only; the schemas already exist in shared types so this is documentation of existing surface, not new API.

findings.subsystem: sdk-integration
findings.title: node-encryption example pulls the entire neon-js + deploy toolchain just to encrypt a payload
findings.evidence: examples/node-encryption/encrypt-oracle-payload.mjs:1 imports encryptWithOracleKey from ../scripts/common.mjs, but common.mjs:6-11 imports @cityofzion/neon-js, loadDotEnv, and lib-neo-signers, and contains spawnSync('npm', ['run','once:relayer']) machinery (line 530). A developer who only wants the pure WebCrypto encrypt step must install/transitively load the full chain/deploy stack. The encrypt primitive itself (common.mjs:430-492) has zero chain dependencies.
findings.fix: Have the node-encryption example import the dependency-free encryptConfidentialEnvelope from @neo-morpheus-oracle/shared (after exporting it per the first finding) rather than from common.mjs. This decouples the simplest integration step from the deploy toolchain.
findings.risk: Low; resolves once the shared export exists. Until then it is correctly noted as coupled.

findings.subsystem: sdk-integration
findings.title: Public oracle endpoints return errors without the machine-readable error_code that apps/web already supports
findings.evidence: apps/web/lib/api-helpers.ts defines apiError(message, code, status) producing {error, error_code} for switchable client handling, but it is used in exactly 1 route while badRequest (plain {error}, no code) is used in 17 — including all public oracle paths: oracle/query/route.ts:29,75, oracle/smart-fetch/route.ts:29,75, attestation/verify/route.ts:8. Integrators must string-match human-readable messages to distinguish 'invalid JSON body' from a provider error.
findings.fix: Adopt apiError with stable codes (e.g. INVALID_BODY, PROVIDER_RESOLVE_FAILED) on the public oracle/compute/attestation routes, and document the code set. Keep the {error} field for backward compat (apiError already includes it).
findings.risk: Low; additive (error_code alongside existing error). Verify no client asserts on exact body shape excluding extra keys.

findings.subsystem: sdk-integration
findings.title: API Reference page has a mislabeled duplicate 'Enclave SDK (Javascript)' heading
findings.evidence:
apps/web/app/docs/api-reference/page.tsx:101 renders `

2. Enclave SDK (Javascript)

` but the section body (lines 105-159) documents the NeoDID resolve endpoint (GET /api/neodid/resolve), not the SDK. Line 161 then renders `

3. Enclave SDK (Javascript)

` which is the actual SDK section. Two identically-titled headings, the first wrong, in the canonical developer reference.
findings.fix: Retitle the section at line 101 to 'NeoDID Resolution (W3C DID)' and renumber so the SDK section is unique.
findings.risk: None; static text fix.

findings.subsystem: security (whole-oracle: web API authn/authz, rate-limiting, secret handling, TEE attestation, fulfillment-digest binding, SSRF/injection lanes, signer key scope)
findings.title: Caller-supplied `signing_key` (and `mnemonic`/`seed`/`passphrase`) private-key material is written in cleartext to the operation log (Supabase + BetterStack)
findings.evidence: apps/web/lib/operation-logs.ts:36 — SENSITIVE_KEY_PATTERN = /(authorization|token|secret|password|private[_-]?key|wif|api[_-]?key)/i. It does NOT match `signing_key`, `mnemonic`, `seed`, or `passphrase`. But `signing_key` is a first-class caller-supplied private-key field accepted by the worker everywhere: workers/nitro-worker/src/chain/signing.js:25,132,194 (`payload.signing_key || payload.wif`), workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/feeds/feed-submit.js:10, workers/nitro-worker/src/neodid/index.js:322, workers/nitro-worker/src/chain/neo-n3.js:92. The admin routes /api/sign/payload (apps/web/app/api/sign/payload/route.ts:54 `requestPayload: parsed`) and /api/relay/transaction (apps/web/app/api/relay/transaction/route.ts:55) forward the full parsed body to recordOperationLog. sanitizeValue() (operation-logs.ts:107-111) only redacts when the *immediate key* matches the pattern, so a body `{ "signing_key": "Kx...WIF..." }` is persisted verbatim into morpheus_operation_logs.request_payload AND mirrored to BetterStack (operation-logs.ts:255). Verified the pattern does not match: node check shows signing_key=false, mnemonic=false, seed=false, passphrase=false. Note the WORKER's own error redaction (workers/nitro-worker/src/platform/core.js:277) DOES include /signing[_ ]?key/i — only the web operation-log redaction is missing it, confirming this is an oversight, not intentional.
findings.fix: In apps/web/lib/operation-logs.ts extend SENSITIVE_KEY_PATTERN to include `signing[_-]?key`, `mnemonic`, `seed[_-]?phrase|seed`, `passphrase`, `credential`, and `signing_seed` (mirror workers/nitro-worker/src/platform/core.js SENSITIVE_PATTERNS). Better: maintain one shared redaction list in @neo-morpheus-oracle/shared and import it in both places so the key vocabulary can never drift again. Add a unit test asserting `{signing_key, mnemonic}` are redacted.
findings.risk: Any private key passed via the `signing_key` (or mnemonic/seed) field — which the API documents/accepts as an alternative to `private_key`/`wif` — is exfiltrated to the operations database and the external BetterStack log sink in cleartext, where DB-read or log access yields a spendable key. Largest blast radius is the relay/sign admin paths.

findings.subsystem: security (whole-oracle: web API authn/authz, rate-limiting, secret handling, TEE attestation, fulfillment-digest binding, SSRF/injection lanes, signer key scope)
findings.title: Admin/operator API-key checks (`/sign/payload`, `/relay/transaction`, relayer-ops, provider-config) use non-constant-time comparison
findings.evidence: apps/web/lib/server-supabase.ts:147 — isAuthorizedAdminRequest compares with `configured.includes(headerKey) || configured.some((value) => bearer === ` + "`Bearer ${value}`" + `)`, i.e. plain `Array.includes`/`===`. This is the guard for the highest-value endpoints: /api/sign/payload (route.ts:37), /api/relay/transaction (route.ts:37), /api/confidential/store (route.ts:59), and all relayer-ops routes via apps/web/lib/relayer-auth.ts:14. The codebase already standardized on timingSafeCompare elsewhere (apps/web/lib/control-plane-auth.ts:18, apps/web/lib/cron-auth.ts:17, deploy/cloudflare/morpheus-control-plane/lib/auth.js:39, workers/nitro-worker/src/platform/auth.js:26) — this one helper was left on `===`/`includes`, so it is an inconsistency, not a design choice.
findings.fix: Rewrite isAuthorizedAdminRequest to compare each candidate with timingSafeCompare(headerKey, value) and timingSafeCompare(bearer, `Bearer ${value}`) (import from @neo-morpheus-oracle/shared/utils, already used by control-plane-auth.ts). Iterate all candidates unconditionally (no early-return on length) to avoid leaking via short-circuit timing.
findings.risk: Remote timing side-channel against the operator API keys protecting signing and on-chain relay. Exploitability over the public internet is reduced by network jitter, but these keys authorize on-chain GAS-spending operations, so the residual risk warrants the same constant-time treatment already applied to the sibling auth helpers.

findings.subsystem: security (whole-oracle: web API authn/authz, rate-limiting, secret handling, TEE attestation, fulfillment-digest binding, SSRF/injection lanes, signer key scope)
findings.title: Edge-gateway trusted-token check is non-constant-time and gates the bypass of Turnstile + rate-limiting
findings.evidence: deploy/cloudflare/morpheus-edge-gateway/worker.mjs:135 — isTrustedAutomationRequest returns `trustedTokens.includes(token)` (plain Array.includes against MORPHEUS_RUNTIME_TOKEN/MORPHEUS_EDGE_RUNTIME_TOKEN/MORPHEUS_ORIGIN_TOKEN/PHALA_API_TOKEN/PHALA_SHARED_SECRET). A valid token here makes the request skip Turnstile (worker.mjs:148 `if (isTrustedAutomationRequest(...)) return null;`), skip native rate-limit (worker.mjs:186) and skip Upstash rate-limit (worker.mjs:213). The shared package exports timingSafeCompare and the control-plane CF worker (deploy/cloudflare/morpheus-control-plane/lib/auth.js:39) already uses it — the edge gateway was not updated.
findings.fix: In deploy/cloudflare/morpheus-edge-gateway/worker.mjs replace the `trustedTokens.includes(token)` with a loop using timingSafeCompare (already imported pattern in the sibling control-plane worker; import from @neo-morpheus-oracle/shared/utils). Evaluate all entries to avoid early-exit timing.
findings.risk: Timing oracle on the gateway-wide bypass token. Guessing it disables the platform's DoS/abuse controls (Turnstile + both rate limiters) for the attacker, turning the public oracle/compute/neodid lanes into an unthrottled enclave-CPU sink.

findings.subsystem: security (whole-oracle: web API authn/authz, rate-limiting, secret handling, TEE attestation, fulfillment-digest binding, SSRF/injection lanes, signer key scope)
findings.title: Public neodid signing/TEE lanes (action-ticket, bind, recovery-ticket, zklogin-ticket) have no app-level rate limiting
findings.evidence: apps/web/app/api/neodid/action-ticket/route.ts:8, bind/route.ts:8, recovery-ticket/route.ts, zklogin-ticket/route.ts each define a bare `export async function POST` that calls proxyToNitro/dispatchToControlPlane with NO createRateLimitedHandler wrapper — unlike the comparable public lanes oracle/query/route.ts:78 (maxRequests 30), compute/execute/route.ts:57 (20), oracle/smart-fetch/route.ts:78 (20), confidential/store/route.ts:187 (20). The web app holds the worker origin token (apps/web/lib/nitro.ts:95-99 injects `Bearer ${appConfig.nitroToken}`), so the worker's requireAuth (workers/nitro-worker/src/worker.js:155) is satisfied for every proxied call and provides no per-client throttle. These handlers perform enclave-bound key derivation + secp256r1 signing (workers/nitro-worker/src/neodid/index.js:320 resolveNeoDidSignerPrivateKey -> deriveNeoN3PrivateKeyHex). The Vercel-hosted web origin is directly reachable, bypassing the edge gateway's Turnstile/rate-limit.
findings.fix: Wrap each neodid POST handler in createRateLimitedHandler with a dedicated scope (e.g. neodid_action_ticket) and a low maxRequests (mirror the 20/min used by compute/execute), matching the pattern already applied to the other public worker-proxy lanes. Apply the same to any other unwrapped public worker-proxy POST (audit pages/app api dir).
findings.risk: Unauthenticated clients can drive unbounded enclave key-derivation/signing work through the public Vercel route, exhausting the worker's overload-guard slots (DoS for legitimate oracle traffic) and inflating operation-log/Supabase storage — the same quota-exhaustion class that previously caused an oracle outage (per project memory).

findings.subsystem: security (whole-oracle: web API authn/authz, rate-limiting, secret handling, TEE attestation, fulfillment-digest binding, SSRF/injection lanes, signer key scope)
findings.title: TEE attestation verifier validates only hash binding, never the Nitro/TDX quote signature chain — attested results are trusted on the strength of the worker's secp256r1 key alone
findings.evidence: apps/web/lib/attestation.ts:202 self-documents: 'Full Intel/TDX quote-chain validation is out of scope for this application-level verifier.' verifyAttestation() only checks that output_hash/attestation_hash/report_data prefixes are mutually consistent (attestation.ts:114-135) and that an opaque `quote`/`event_log` field is present (lines 158-160 hasQuote/hasEventLog are mere presence checks, not cryptographic verification). Nothing verifies the quote is signed by a genuine AWS Nitro / Intel TDX root, nor that compose_hash/app_id match a pinned expected enclave measurement unless the *caller* supplies expectedComposeHash/expectedAppId (attestation.ts:138-144, optional). So `full_attestation_ok` can be true for an entirely fabricated `quote` string with self-consistent hashes. The actual integrity guarantee reduces to: the result is signed by the configured oracle_verifier key (workers/morpheus-relayer/src/fulfillment.js:288 callNitro /sign/payload), which is the same trust the on-chain digest already provides.
findings.fix: Document explicitly (capability registry + verification envelope `note`) that attestation today is 'enclave-key-signed result + presence-only quote', NOT a verified TDX/Nitro measurement chain, so downstream consumers don't over-trust full_attestation_ok. If genuine TEE assurance is a product requirement, add real NSM/TDX quote signature + measurement verification (deploy/nitro/nsm-attest exists as the seam) and pin expectedComposeHash/expectedAppId server-side rather than accepting them from the caller. At minimum, make the public /api/attestation/verify response surface that the quote chain was not validated so a green check is not misread.
findings.risk: Consumers (or the dashboard) may treat `full_attestation_ok: true` as proof the result came from a genuine, measured enclave when it only proves internal hash consistency + a present-but-unverified quote blob. If the oracle_verifier signing key is ever compromised, attestation provides no additional defense. Real exploitability is gated on key compromise / a consumer that relies on the quote, hence medium not high.

findings.subsystem: security (whole-oracle: web API authn/authz, rate-limiting, secret handling, TEE attestation, fulfillment-digest binding, SSRF/injection lanes, signer key scope)
findings.title: Recipient-reveal lane has no per-message replay nonce within the 10-minute freshness window
findings.evidence: workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/message-reveal.js:19 REVEAL_FRESHNESS_WINDOW_SECONDS=600 with ±window check at isRevealTimestampFresh (line 63-71); the signed statement (buildRevealStatement line 34-43) binds chain+contract+messageId+issuedAt but there is no seen-signature/nonce store. A captured (chain,contract,messageId,issuedAt,signature) tuple is replayable for up to ~20 minutes (±10 min). Contrast workers/nitro-worker/src/chain/signing.js:83-103 which maintains seenRequestIds replay protection for the signing lane.
findings.fix: Low priority because the only effect of replay is that the *same authenticated recipient* (the address that produced a valid signature) re-reads their own plaintext over the authenticated, token-gated HTTPS lane — no privilege escalation. If tightening: shorten the window, and/or remember (messageId, sha256(signature)) for the window to reject exact replays. Worth a one-line code comment noting the deliberate replay tolerance so it is not mistaken for a gap.
findings.risk: Minimal — replay only re-serves plaintext to a party who already authenticated as the recipient. Recorded as a low-severity completeness note, not an exploitable confidentiality break.

findings.subsystem: observability-ops (morpheus-relayer metrics/logging/health, nitro-worker health, box systemd/monitoring, callback/feed monitors)
findings.title: Dead-lettered (permanently lost) oracle callback fires no alert hook — only a log line + counter
findings.evidence: workers/morpheus-relayer/src/fulfillment.js:783-838 recordDeliveryExhaustion is the terminal path when a callback exhausts its retry ceiling (a lost/undelivered oracle result). It only: incrementMetric(state,'retries_exhausted_total') (794), upserts durable status 'exhausted' (813), and writes ONE logger.error 'Callback delivery retries exhausted; dead-lettered...' (823). The failure heartbeat (config.js:548 MORPHEUS_BETTERSTACK_RELAYER_FAILURE_URL) is fired ONLY on a whole-tick crash (relayer.js:256), never on dead-letter. So the single most important incident — a request whose callback will never be delivered — produces no dedicated push alert. An operator only learns of it if they happen to alert on a counter delta or grep logs.
findings.fix: In recordDeliveryExhaustion, after persistState(), fire a dedicated heartbeat/alert: void sendHeartbeat(config.heartbeats.failure || config.heartbeats.deadLetter, {kind:'dead_letter', chain, request_id, request_type, module_id, operation, attempts, error}). Add a config key MORPHEUS_BETTERSTACK_RELAYER_DEADLETTER_URL so dead-letters route to a higher-severity Better Stack monitor / PagerDuty than generic tick failures.
findings.risk: Best-effort fire-and-forget (already the heartbeat pattern); no money-path behavior change. Low risk.

findings.subsystem: observability-ops (morpheus-relayer metrics/logging/health, nitro-worker health, box systemd/monitoring, callback/feed monitors)
findings.title: No delivery-latency or queue-age metric — operator cannot distinguish 'slow but recovering' from 'permanently stuck'
findings.evidence: request_age_ms (request-created → processing-started) is computed in fulfillment.js:1011-1014 and only LOGGED (1034,1107,1149) — never recorded as a metric. prometheus.js exports retry_queue_sizes and dead_letter_sizes as COUNTS only (state.js:174-175 reads .length). Nothing exports the AGE of the oldest retry item (retry_queue[].first_failed_at exists in state.js:222 but is never aggregated) nor end-to-end fulfillment latency. Result: an operator scraping /metrics sees 'retry_queue_size{chain=neo_n3}=12' but cannot tell whether those 12 are seconds-old (normal backoff) or hours-old (a wedged callback contract). There is no SLO-able 'time-to-deliver' signal.
findings.fix: In snapshotMetrics (state.js:166) compute, per chain, oldest_retry_age_ms = now - min(first_failed_at) and oldest_dead_letter_age_ms = now - min(exhausted_at), and emit them in prometheus.js as gauges morpheus_relayer_oldest_retry_age_seconds{chain} / morpheus_relayer_oldest_dead_letter_age_seconds{chain}. Add a delivery-latency gauge: on success in processEvent record last_fulfill_latency_ms (=request_age_ms at completion) into metrics and export it. These three gauges make 'a callback is stuck' a one-query alert.
findings.risk: Pure read-side aggregation over existing state fields; no write-path change. Low risk.

findings.subsystem: observability-ops (morpheus-relayer metrics/logging/health, nitro-worker health, box systemd/monitoring, callback/feed monitors)
findings.title: Failure counters are flat globals with no chain/module/operation labels — can't localize an incident
findings.evidence: All failure/retry counters in state.js:28-59 (worker_failures_total, fulfill_failure_total, retries_exhausted_total, claim_conflicts_total) are single scalars, and prometheus.js:46-49 emits them label-free (morpheus_relayer_retries_exhausted_total <n>). Yet every log line and every dead_letter record (state.js:264-274) already carries chain + module_id + operation. So when retries_exhausted_total rises, an operator cannot tell from metrics whether it's one app's broken callback contract (one operation), one chain (neox RPC down), or a systemic worker outage — they must drop to log-grep. Only retry/dead-letter SIZES are per-chain (prometheus.js:71-79).
findings.fix: Promote the high-signal failure counters to labeled maps in state.js (e.g. fulfill_failure_total_by_module[`${chain}:${moduleId}:${operation}`]) and emit them in prometheus.js with {chain,module,operation} labels, mirroring the existing per-chain retry_queue_size loop. Keep the flat totals for backward-compat dashboards. Cardinality is bounded by the finite module/operation set.
findings.risk: Additive metrics; existing scalar counters preserved. Low correctness risk; watch label cardinality (bounded here).

findings.subsystem: observability-ops (morpheus-relayer metrics/logging/health, nitro-worker health, box systemd/monitoring, callback/feed monitors)
findings.title: Relayer systemd unit has no liveness probe — a wedged-but-alive tick loop is never restarted
findings.evidence: deploy/systemd/morpheus-relayer.service uses Type=simple + Restart=always (lines 7,11) — which only restarts on process EXIT, not on a hung loop. healthcheck.js (checkRelayerStateFreshness, detects last_tick_completed_at staleness > MORPHEUS_RELAYER_HEALTH_MAX_STALE_MS) IS wired into the docker path (deploy/nitro/docker-compose.request-hub.yml:162,201) but NOT into any systemd unit — the prompt states the box runs relayer/worker/feed-pusher as systemd units. runRelayerOnce (relayer.js:135) awaits chain scans with no outer timeout, so a stuck RPC fetch inside a tick hangs the loop indefinitely while the process stays alive and systemd does nothing. (Note: docker's restart:unless-stopped also does NOT act on 'unhealthy' without an autoheal sidecar, so the docker probe is informational too.)
findings.fix: Add systemd watchdog wiring: set WatchdogSec=180 on morpheus-relayer.service and have the loop call sd_notify WATCHDOG=1 after each successful tick (via the systemd-notify / sd-notify npm shim, Type=notify), OR add a sibling oneshot+timer that runs `node workers/morpheus-relayer/src/healthcheck.js` every 60s and `systemctl restart morpheus-relayer` on non-zero exit. healthcheck.js already returns the right exit code (line 58-61).
findings.risk: Watchdog restart on a genuinely-wedged loop is the desired behavior; tune WatchdogSec above worst-case tick duration to avoid flapping. Low risk if threshold is conservative.

findings.subsystem: observability-ops (morpheus-relayer metrics/logging/health, nitro-worker health, box systemd/monitoring, callback/feed monitors)
findings.title: metrics-server /healthz always returns 200 — useless as a real health probe for uptime checks
findings.evidence: metrics-server.js:39-52 — GET /healthz unconditionally writes 200 {status:'ok'}. It does NOT call checkRelayerStateFreshness (healthcheck.js:8) even though that function exists and is the canonical staleness check. The Checkly scaffold and Grafana README (monitoring/grafana/README.md:10) point operators at this endpoint as 'health', but it reports green even when the relayer tick loop is dead/stale. An external HTTP uptime check on /healthz can never detect a wedged relayer.
findings.fix: In metrics-server.js make /healthz call checkRelayerStateFreshness({stateFile: config.stateFile, maxStaleMs: config.healthMaxStaleMs}) and return 503 with the {reason, age_ms} body when result.ok===false, 200 otherwise. Reuse the exact function the docker healthcheck uses so all three paths (docker, external HTTP, CLI) agree.
findings.risk: Changes /healthz semantics — confirm any existing check that asserts 200 unconditionally is updated; that is the intended fix, not a regression.

findings.subsystem: observability-ops (morpheus-relayer metrics/logging/health, nitro-worker health, box systemd/monitoring, callback/feed monitors)
findings.title: nitro-worker /health is a static 'ok' — no readiness signal for signing key / enclave / feed freshness
findings.evidence: workers/nitro-worker/src/worker.js:32-45 handleHealth() returns a hardcoded {status:'ok', features:...} with zero runtime introspection. It does not check whether the signing key is loaded, whether the dstack/enclave attestation socket is reachable (getDstackInfo exists and is used by /info at worker.js:139), or whether the price-feed cache is stale. The relayer (nitro-signer.js:180) and the public /api/runtime/health (route.ts proxyToNitro('/health')) and the Checkly 'oracle-runtime-health' check all trust this endpoint as readiness — so a worker that started but cannot sign or attest still reports healthy, masking a real outage until requests start failing.
findings.fix: Make handleHealth() probe the critical dependencies and degrade: include {signing_key_loaded: bool, dstack: await getDstackInfo({required:false}) reachable?, feeds_stale_count}. Return 503 (or status:'degraded') when the signing key is missing or attestation is required-but-unreachable, so /api/runtime/health and Checkly trip on a truly unhealthy worker.
findings.risk: Must keep /health unauthenticated and cheap (no expensive per-call enclave round-trip — cache the dstack reachability). Tightening readiness could surface pre-existing flakiness; gate strict checks behind required flags as the codebase already does.

findings.subsystem: observability-ops (morpheus-relayer metrics/logging/health, nitro-worker health, box systemd/monitoring, callback/feed monitors)
findings.title: No metric/visibility for Better Stack log-sink drops — silent loss of error logs under load or outage
findings.evidence: betterstack-log-sink.js silently drops logs in two places with no counter: (1) line 73 `if (queueLocked) return;` drops every log enqueued during a flush batch-extract window; (2) lines 80-82 splice the queue down to maxQueue (default 500) when full, discarding the OLDEST records. flush() also swallows all POST failures (line 48 empty catch). So during an incident — exactly when error log volume spikes and the network may be degraded — the relayer's error logs to the central sink are dropped with zero indication, and there's no metric an operator can alert on to know their log pipeline lost data.
findings.fix: Maintain a module-local dropped counter (increment in both drop branches and on flush() catch) and surface it: either log it periodically to stdout (which still goes to journald), or expose it via a getter that state/snapshotMetrics emits as morpheus_relayer_log_sink_dropped_total. At minimum, drop NEWEST-on-overflow for error-level records or reserve headroom so errors survive a warn/info flood.
findings.risk: Counter is additive; changing overflow policy needs care to not unbound memory. Low risk.

findings.subsystem: observability-ops (morpheus-relayer metrics/logging/health, nitro-worker health, box systemd/monitoring, callback/feed monitors)
findings.title: No operator tooling or runbook to inspect/replay dead-letters — recovery requires hand-editing the state file
findings.evidence: cli.js (the relayer CLI) exposes only loop / metrics / metrics:prom / serve:metrics / once (lines 8-29) — there is NO command to list dead_letters, show a specific stuck request, or requeue one for replay. Dead-letters live in state[chain].dead_letters (state.js:264) and in Supabase, but the only replay mechanism is the automatic finalize path. docs/OPERATIONS.md:114-120 'Recovery Checklist' says 'recover stale jobs instead of replaying blindly' but gives no command and there is no dead-letter replay runbook (the only RUNBOOKs are feed-pusher and evm; the dead-letter error message even says 'for manual replay' at fulfillment.js:837 with no documented how-to). The log message promises a manual replay path that does not exist as tooling.
findings.fix: Add cli.js subcommands: `dead-letters [chain]` (print state[chain].dead_letters with age + last_error), and `replay <chain> <event_key>` (move a dead_letter back into retry_queue with attempts reset, via enqueueRetryItem). Document the procedure in a docs/runbooks/relayer-dead-letter-replay.md referenced from OPERATIONS.md and from the exhaustion log message.
findings.risk: Replay re-delivers a callback — must reuse the existing idempotency/claim guards so a replayed request can't double-deliver; gate behind explicit event_key, never bulk-replay-all.

findings.subsystem: observability-ops (morpheus-relayer metrics/logging/health, nitro-worker health, box systemd/monitoring, callback/feed monitors)
findings.title: Feed staleness is not a relayer metric — only an out-of-band external monitor checks it
findings.evidence: The relayer tracks last_feed_sync_success_at (state.js:52, exported prometheus.js:60) — i.e. when the relayer last RAN a feed sync — but never the on-chain feed AGE (how stale the published price actually is). The only staleness check is deploy/feed-pusher/feed-monitor.mjs, a separate oneshot that hardcodes a single pair ('TWELVEDATA:NEO-USD', line 58) and one feed contract (line 9) and writes a local JSON/log; it does not feed Prometheus and does not cover the full feed set. So Grafana/Prometheus dashboards have no per-pair feed-age series, and the one staleness monitor is a single-pair script the metrics stack can't alert on uniformly.
findings.fix: Emit a per-pair on-chain feed-age gauge from the relayer feed-sync path (it already reads publication summaries — relayer.js:237 publication_summary) as morpheus_relayer_feed_age_seconds{pair,network}, OR have feed-monitor.mjs iterate the full pair registry and write a Prometheus textfile (node_exporter textfile collector) instead of a bespoke JSON. Either gives one alertable feed-staleness signal across all pairs.
findings.risk: Reads only; adding per-pair labels is bounded by the pair set. Low risk.

findings.subsystem: observability-ops (morpheus-relayer metrics/logging/health, nitro-worker health, box systemd/monitoring, callback/feed monitors)
findings.title: request_age_ms is meaningless on the Neo N3 path because createdAtMs is sourced from a contract field that may be a block height, and blockNumber is set equal to it
findings.evidence: neo-n3.js:276-278 and 314-316 set both createdAtMs: Number(createdAtMs||0) AND blockNumber: Number(createdAtMs||0) from the SAME decoded contract field. If that field is a block index (not an epoch-ms timestamp), then request_age_ms = now - createdAtMs (fulfillment.js:1013) is garbage (now is epoch-ms, createdAtMs would be ~a few million), producing absurd latency log values and corrupting any future latency metric built on it. The neox path correctly multiplies seconds*1000 (neox.js:176), highlighting the N3 path's ambiguity. blockNumber being aliased to createdAtMs also means buildEventKey's blockNumber component is not a real block height for N3.
findings.fix: Verify the decoded N3 field semantics; if it is a millisecond timestamp keep it but stop aliasing blockNumber to it (set a real block height or 0); if it is a block index, derive createdAtMs from the block timestamp (or set createdAtMs:null so request_age_ms stays null rather than wrong). Add a unit assertion in neo-n3 decode tests that createdAtMs is in a plausible epoch-ms range before it is used for latency.
findings.risk: Only affects observability fields, not delivery — but a downstream latency metric (finding #2) would inherit the bug, so fix before adding it.

findings.subsystem: onchain-contracts (Neo N3 contracts/ + Neo X contracts-evm/)
findings.title: EVM dice bankroll: placeBet solvency check ignores totalPending, can strand pending-withdrawal claimants
findings.evidence: contracts-evm/MiniAppDiceGameEVM.sol:106 — placeBet guards `if (address(this).balance < reserved + payout) revert InsufficientBankroll();`. This omits `totalPending` (funds already owed to claimants whose push payout failed). But availableBankroll() (line 94-98) and withdrawBankroll() (line 193) DO subtract `reserved + totalPending`. The two functions disagree on what bankroll is free. A bet can therefore be admitted that encroaches on funds owed to pendingWithdrawals; if that bet then wins, _pay (line 168-172) sends the full payout and can leave balance < totalPending, making a claimant's claim() (line 175-183) revert for lack of balance until the bankroll is topped up.
findings.fix: Change the line 106 guard to `if (address(this).balance < reserved + totalPending + payout) revert InsufficientBankroll();` so it matches availableBankroll()'s definition of free funds. Needs an EVM redeploy + consumer repoint (no proxy).
findings.risk: Behavior change makes placeBet slightly stricter; with fee=0 and rare push-failures the practical exposure is small, but it closes an accounting inconsistency that can temporarily lock claimant funds. Redeploy + re-register required.

findings.subsystem: onchain-contracts (Neo N3 contracts/ + Neo X contracts-evm/)
findings.title: EVM MorpheusPriceFeed has no admin reset escape hatch — one bad roundId permanently stalls a symbol
findings.evidence: contracts-evm/MorpheusPriceFeed.sol:77 enforces strict roundId monotonicity (`if (f.roundId != 0 && roundIds_[i] <= f.roundId) revert StaleRound`). There is no admin override (only setUpdater/setOwner at lines 52/58). The N3 mirror explicitly added MorpheusDataFeed.AdminResetFeed (contracts/MorpheusDataFeed/MorpheusDataFeed.cs:191) precisely because 'a single bad publish (a far-future timestamp or an oversized roundId) would otherwise permanently reject every subsequent legitimate update and stall the feed.' The EVM side is missing this recovery path, so a buggy/compromised updater pushing roundId=type(uint256).max for a symbol freezes that feed forever with no recovery short of full redeploy. The contracts-evm/README.md next-deployment notes do NOT track this gap.
findings.fix: Add an owner-only `adminResetFeed(symbol, price, timestamp, roundId)` to MorpheusPriceFeed that bypasses the StaleRound guard (mirror of N3 AdminResetFeed), restricted to onlyOwner so producers cannot rewind/replay. Needs an EVM redeploy.
findings.risk: Adds an admin-trusted write that bypasses monotonicity; owner is already fully trusted (can setUpdater). Lower exposure than its benefit. Redeploy required.

findings.subsystem: onchain-contracts (Neo N3 contracts/ + Neo X contracts-evm/)
findings.title: EVM MorpheusOracleEVM has no request expiry/refund — asymmetric with N3 ExpireStaleRequest
findings.evidence: The N3 kernel has a full expiry+refund lifecycle (contracts/MorpheusOracle/MorpheusOracle.cs:667 ExpireStaleRequest: refunds FeePaid to the sponsor and releases the reserve after RequestTTL). MorpheusOracleEVM.sol has no equivalent: a Pending request that the relayer never fulfills stays Pending forever, and its requestFee (line 131 `accruedFees += requestFee`) is kept by the owner with no refund path. There is no TTL, no expire, no requester refund. With the operationally-pinned requestFee=0 (per README) the fund impact is currently nil, but the asymmetry means any future non-zero fee makes unfulfilled requests a silent fee sink with no user recourse.
findings.fix: If a non-zero EVM requestFee is ever enabled, add an `expireRequest(requestId)` (after a configurable TTL) that sets status=Failed and refunds the requester's paid fee, mirroring N3. Until then, keep requestFee pinned at 0 (already an operational note) and document the absence. Needs an EVM redeploy to add.
findings.risk: Pure addition; only relevant once a fee is charged. Low urgency given fee=0 today.

findings.subsystem: onchain-contracts (Neo N3 contracts/ + Neo X contracts-evm/)
findings.title: N3 MorpheusDataFeed exposes no on-chain Decimals() — consumers must hardcode the 1e6 scale (100x-error hazard)
findings.evidence: contracts/MorpheusDataFeed/MorpheusDataFeed.cs stores Price as a bare BigInteger (FeedRecord, line 45-53) with no decimals metadata and no Decimals()/description() view method. The feed-pusher writes N3 prices at 1e6 scale (deploy/feed-pusher/feed-pusher.mjs:248 `Math.round(prices[s] * 1e6)`), but a Neo N3 consumer has no on-chain way to discover that scale and could reasonably assume 1e8 (the GAS/NEP-17 convention used elsewhere in this codebase, e.g. feed-pusher.mjs:214 reads a different source at /1e8), yielding a 100x price error. The EVM mirror at least exposes `uint8 public constant DECIMALS = 6` (MorpheusPriceFeed.sol:10).
findings.fix: Add a `[Safe] public static BigInteger Decimals() => 6;` (or a per-pair decimals field on FeedRecord set by the updater) to MorpheusDataFeed so consumers can read the scale on-chain, matching the EVM DECIMALS surface. Needs an N3 redeploy.
findings.risk: Redeploy of the data-feed contract + consumer awareness; a constant Decimals() is a safe additive change. Hash changes, so consumers must repoint.

findings.subsystem: onchain-contracts (Neo N3 contracts/ + Neo X contracts-evm/)
findings.title: Neither oracle contract caps the signed result to its on-chain MAX_RESULT_LENGTH before signing — latent stuck-request risk
findings.evidence: contracts/MorpheusOracle/MorpheusOracle.cs:926 asserts `result.Length <= MAX_RESULT_LENGTH` (4096) in FulfillRequest, but the off-chain signer (workers/morpheus-relayer/src/router.js:302 buildFulfillmentDigestBytes) hashes the result with no length cap. If any worker lane emits a result > 4096 bytes, the relayer produces a valid signature whose FulfillRequest reverts 'result too large', stranding the request until TTL-driven ExpireStaleRequest. Today this is mitigated off-chain: buildOnchainResultEnvelope caps the JSON envelope to ~900 bytes (router.js:577) and resolveCompactCallbackBytes uses 255-byte-prefixed segments (router.js:31), so in practice on-chain results stay small. The contract itself provides no defensive guarantee, so the mitigation is purely a property of the current router code, not a contract invariant.
findings.fix: Belt-and-suspenders: have the relayer assert resultBytes.length <= 4096 (and error.length <= 256) before signing in fulfillment.js/router.js so an oversized result fails fast with a clear error and a finalize-failed result instead of producing an unfulfillable signature. (Off-chain fix; no redeploy.) The contract bound is correct as-is.
findings.risk: Off-chain guard only; verify the 900-byte/255-byte caps truly bound every lane (vrf/compute/decrypt/http) before relying on the current mitigation.

findings.subsystem: onchain-contracts (Neo N3 contracts/ + Neo X contracts-evm/)
findings.title: N3 SubmitMiniAppRequestInternal deserializes the MiniApp record twice and re-checks Active redundantly
findings.evidence: contracts/MorpheusOracle/MorpheusOracle.cs:1320-1323 — SubmitMiniAppRequestInternal calls ValidateRequestInputs(appId,...) which already does RequireActiveMiniApp(appId) (line 1110, full AppMap().Get + StdLib.Deserialize) and asserts app.Active (line 1115), then immediately calls RequireActiveMiniApp(appId) AGAIN at line 1323 — a second storage read + deserialize of the same ~9-field struct per request. Within ValidateRequestInputs lines 1115-1116 also re-assert app.Active/module.Active that RequireActiveMiniApp/RequireActiveModule already enforced.
findings.fix: Have ValidateRequestInputs return the deserialized MiniAppRecord (or inline it) so SubmitMiniAppRequestInternal reuses the app instead of re-reading it; drop the duplicate app.Active assertion. Saves one StorageMap read + deserialize of GAS per request. Needs an N3 redeploy to take effect.
findings.risk: Behavior-preserving refactor; the C# contracts are bytecode-frozen so this only lands on the next redeploy. Pin with the existing fee-accounting VM tests.

findings.subsystem: onchain-contracts (Neo N3 contracts/ + Neo X contracts-evm/)
findings.title: EVM oracle _recover does not enforce low-s (signature malleability) — defensive gap, not currently exploitable
findings.evidence: contracts-evm/MorpheusOracleEVM.sol:187-194 _recover validates only sig.length==65 and v∈{27,28}; it does not reject high-s values (s > secp256k1n/2), so two distinct valid signatures exist for one digest. It is NOT exploitable for replay here because fulfillRequest gates on `status != Pending` (line 157) so a request can only be fulfilled once regardless of which malleable form is presented, and the digest binds chainid+address+requestId. Still, omitting the OpenZeppelin-standard low-s check is a defensive gap if this _recover is ever reused where signature uniqueness matters.
findings.fix: Add the standard `if (uint256(s) > 0x7FFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF5D576E7357A4501DDFE92F46681B20A0) return address(0);` low-s guard in _recover (mirror OZ ECDSA). Needs an EVM redeploy; low priority given status-gating already prevents replay.
findings.risk: Pure hardening; no behavior change for honest signers. Redeploy required.

findings.subsystem: onchain-contracts (Neo N3 contracts/ + Neo X contracts-evm/)
findings.title: EVM contracts (oracle kernel, dice, message, price feed) have no Solidity unit tests — money/fulfill paths only validated against live testnet
findings.evidence: No Foundry/Hardhat harness exists (no *.t.sol, foundry.toml, or hardhat.config in the repo; only deploy/evm/validate-*.mjs scripts that hit live Neo X). The N3 kernel by contrast has thorough VM-level tests (contracts/__tests__/MorpheusOracleFeeAccountingTests.cs exercises submit→fulfill→expire, the reserve invariant, the secp256r1 digest, and AssertSolvent). The EVM kernel's fee accounting, ecrecover digest binding (fulfillmentDigest line 144), best-effort callback non-revert, and the dice bankroll reservation/solvency math (placeBet/_settle/_pay) — all on the EVM money path — have zero unit coverage, so regressions (e.g. the placeBet totalPending bug above, or a digest-encoding drift vs the relayer's signNeoXFulfillment) are only catchable on-chain after deploy.
findings.fix: Add a minimal Foundry suite under contracts-evm/test/ covering: fulfillRequest digest+ecrecover happy path and BadSignature, double-fulfill rejection, requestFromCallback routing, dice placeBet reservation accounting incl. totalPending, settleFromKernel recovery, and StaleRound on the price feed. This is the EVM analogue of the C# fee-accounting tests and gates the asymmetries flagged here.
findings.risk: Test-only; no contract change. The payoff is catching the EVM-side regressions the README's 'no upgrade path' makes expensive to fix post-deploy.

findings.subsystem: onchain-contracts (Neo N3 contracts/ + Neo X contracts-evm/)
findings.title: Neither kernel contract has a fulfillment pause/circuit-breaker; recovery on key compromise relies solely on rotation
findings.evidence: No pause/halt flag exists on either kernel (grep for pause/halt/emergency over MorpheusOracle.cs and MorpheusOracleEVM.sol returns nothing). If the verifier or updater key is compromised, the only mitigation is rotation (N3 SetRuntimeVerificationPublicKey:594 / SetUpdater:564; EVM setOracleVerifier:78 / setUpdater:77), which works but cannot halt in-flight abuse until the rotation tx lands, and cannot stop new submissions from draining prepaid credit. A request-submission/fulfillment pause would give the admin an immediate stop while investigating.
findings.fix: Add an admin-only `paused` flag gating SubmitMiniAppRequest*/FulfillRequest (N3) and submitRequest/fulfillRequest (EVM) for an emergency stop. Lower priority than rotation since rotation already covers the primary key-leak case. Needs redeploys on both chains.
findings.risk: Adds an admin power that could itself be abused to censor; weigh against the rotation-only status quo. Pure addition, redeploy required.

verified.title: Transient worker HTTP 5xx/429 is finalized as a permanent on-chain failure callback instead of being retried
verified.fix_correction:
The fix's intent, placement, and safety are correct, but the stated routing MECHANISM is wrong and must be corrected before implementing.

Claim in the fix: throwing `worker request failed with status ${status} (transient)` makes "the 'transient'/status keywords route it through classifyError -> scheduleRetry". That is only partly true. classifyError (fulfillment.js:84-97) does NOT recognize the word 'transient' as a keyword. Its transient branch matches only: etimedout, econnrefused, econnreset, 'rate limit', 'socket hang up', '503', '502', '504', 'network', 'timed out', 'unavailable'. So:
- 503/502/504 -> the status substring matches -> 'transient' (works).
- 429, 408, 425, 500, 0 -> NO substring in the transient list AND none in the permanent list ('not found'/fault/unauthorized/forbidden/invalid) -> classifyError returns 'unknown', NOT 'transient'.

Why it still happens to work: the worker call lives in prepareOracleFulfillment, which runs BEFORE preparedForRedelivery is set, so a throw lands in processEvent's final catch arm (fulfillment.js:1262-1266). There forceDead is true ONLY when errorClass === 'permanent'; 'unknown' is not permanent, so it goes to scheduleRetry and IS retried (up to config.maxRetries, then finalizeFailedRequest delivers the failure). So the net behavior (retry then eventually finalize) is achieved for all the listed statuses, but NOT via the 'transient' path the fix describes.

Recommended cleaner fix: instead of relying on a 'transient' keyword, extend classifyError's transient branch to recognize the retryable statuses (add 429/'rate limit'/408/425/500/0 and ideally the literal token 'transient'), OR craft the thrown message to contain an already-recognized token (e.g. include 'unavailable' or 'rate limit'). Then the classification is explicit and self-documenting rather than depending on the substring-440 coincidence and the fresh-path 'unknown' fallthrough. Keep deterministic 4xx (400/401/403/404/409/422) on the deliver-as-failure path (encodeFulfillmentResult unchanged; the existing direct test at relayer.test.mjs:842 uses status 400 and must keep asserting success:false). Apply the same status gate to the /oracle/decrypt branch (fulfillment.js:682-696). The requested unit test (503 retries, 400 finalizes) is appropriate; also add a 429/500 case to catch the classification gap above.
verified.reason:
Verified all cited code. The defect is real exactly as described. nitro.js:91 returns {ok:false,status} for ANY HTTP response; only network/timeout throws (nitro.js:100-109). prepareOracleFulfillment (fulfillment.js:728-730) passes workerResponse straight to encodeFulfillmentResult, which on !ok yields {success:false,error:'worker request failed with status N'} (router.js:586-594). processEvent (fulfillment.js:1068-1091) delivers that as fulfillRequest(success=false) inside the try block with no throw, so it is recorded 'failed' (terminal) and never retried. On-chain FulfillRequest (MorpheusOracle.cs:925,948-949) sets Status=Failed + FulfilledAt=Runtime.Time and a second fulfill asserts 'request already fulfilled'; the relayer scan guard (neo-n3.js:261-263) then skips already-settled requests forever. The asymmetry is confirmed: the SAME error at network level (callNitro throws) classifies transient (fulfillment.js:90-95) and retries, while at worker-response level it is burned to a hard failure. The decrypt lane (fulfillment.js:682-696) has the identical defect.

Severity high is defensible: a transient enclave overload, an upstream 429, or a transient 502/503/504 irreversibly burns a permanent on-chain failure callback to consumers on the live price/compute/privacy/confidential-reveal paths (including fee-paid/staked flows), while every other transient lane is protected. It leans high/medium because the consumer still receives a definitive (if premature) failure callback rather than silent loss, and it requires a transient upstream/worker failure to coincide with an in-flight request.

The fix is safe and would not regress existing tests (the only direct encodeFulfillmentResult test uses 400, which the fix preserves; no test asserts the current 5xx->immediate-failure processEvent behavior). Mis-gating a genuinely permanent worker failure as transient only delays the failure callback by the retry ceiling (bounded by resolveCallbackRetryCeiling/maxRetries) before dead-lettering/finalizing — strictly safer than burn-on-first-transient. However, fix_ok is false because the proposed routing mechanism is technically incorrect: classifyError does not treat the word 'transient' as a keyword, and statuses 429/500/408/425/0 classify as 'unknown' (not 'transient'); the retry still occurs only because the fresh-event path retries everything that is not 'permanent'. The fix works but for a different reason than stated and should be implemented by extending classifyError rather than relying on a non-matching keyword. Key files: /Users/jinghuiliao/git/r3e/neo-morpheus-oracle/workers/morpheus-relayer/src/nitro.js, .../src/fulfillment.js, .../src/router.js, .../src/neo-n3.js, .../src/state.js, and /Users/jinghuiliao/git/r3e/neo-morpheus-oracle/contracts/MorpheusOracle/MorpheusOracle.cs.

verified.title: Same on-chain request gets two different event keys across discovery paths, causing cross-tick double-processing in default mainnet block_cursor mode
verified.fix_correction: Fix as described (requestId-aware reconcile exclusion) is correct, targeted, and safe. Implementation notes for whoever applies it: (1) Build the exclusion Set from BOTH sources — retry_queue items expose state[chain].retry_queue[i].event.requestId, and processed_records expose record.request_id (state.js:253 stores request_id:String(event.requestId||'0')); union them with the existing same-tick observedRequestIds and apply at request-processor.js:89-90. (2) Normalize id stringification consistently: observedRequestIds/pendingOnly use String(event.requestId||'') while processed_records store '0' for missing ids — irrelevant for real positive request ids but normalize to avoid a mismatch. (3) CRITICAL safety property the fix preserves: a request the block scan genuinely missed appears in neither retry_queue nor processed_records, so it is NOT excluded and reconcile still picks it up — do not switch to excluding by 'last_request_id range' or 'ever seen', only by retry/processed membership. (4) Bound: reconcile never re-scans ids <= last_request_id (resolveRequestCursor returns lastRequestId+1), so the K2 window only exists while the request cursor lags the block cursor; the membership filter covers exactly that window. (5) Ship a regression test — there is currently ZERO test coverage of reconcilePendingRequests/observedRequestIds dedup, so the bug is untested and the fix would also be untested without one. The alternative (stabilizing buildEventKey to chain:requestId) is correctly flagged as broader/riskier: event_key is the persisted Supabase relayer_jobs key (queue.js, 11 references) plus retry/processed-record keys, so changing it orphans in-flight durable jobs and persisted rows; prefer the reconcile-filter fix.
verified.reason: All cited code verified true. buildEventKey hashes chain:requestId:txHash:logIndex:blockNumber (state.js:135-143). Block/notification scan attaches real txHash/logIndex/blockNumber (neo-n3.js:359-361, 409-412), while the request-record scan used by reconcile sets txHash='',logIndex=0,blockNumber=createdAtMs (neo-n3.js:278-280,316-318) -> different keys (K1 vs K2) for the same requestId. Mainnet default scanMode='block_cursor' (config.js:444-446) routes to processChain (relayer.js:156-168), which runs BOTH the block scan and reconcilePendingRequests via scanByRequestId in the same tick (request-processor.js:202). reconcile only excludes the same-tick observedRequestIds (request-processor.js:89-90, populated from deferred.processable at 175-177), and filterNewEvents dedups by key not requestId (47-67). isEventQueuedForRetry/hasProcessedEvent are key-scoped (state.js:188-201), so when only K1 is in retry_queue, K2 passes both checks. processEvent has no requestId-level idempotency guard: it claims the durable job by event_key (fulfillment.js:1039 -> queue.js:333,339, so K1/K2 are SEPARATE durable jobs and the cross-instance claim does not help), then issues a worker call (prepareOracleFulfillment, 1069-1070) and an on-chain fulfill (1089). For random.generate this recomputes a fresh 32-byte CSPRNG value each time (fulfillment.js:655). The on-chain 'already fulfilled' guard (classifyError->'settled', fulfillment.js:56-82,1158-1168) prevents an actual double callback or fund loss, so impact = wasted worker calls + duplicate processing round + inflated metrics, matching the finding's risk note. Severity high is justified: it is a correctness/robustness defect on the default mainnet money/callback discovery path, reachable in steady state whenever the independent block and request cursors are out of lockstep, and entirely uncovered by tests. One minor wording overstatement: K1 does eventually converge (it terminates as 'settled' via its own retry, clearing the item) rather than 'never converging' — the durable/retry entries do not accumulate forever; the true cost is the extra wasted worker call(s) per affected request. This refinement does not change the verdict.

verified.title: Backoff-skipped retry items are deleted from local state and lost during a multi-instance Supabase outage
verified.fix_correction: The discriminated-result fix is correct and regression-safe (one production caller at fulfillment.js:1039 + one test file; default single-instance path and genuine-conflict path are both preserved). Two refinements: (1) when re-enqueuing on backoff_skip, do NOT increment attempts — bump only next_retry_at — so you don't burn the maxRetries/callback ceiling on a pure infra defer; the finding's wording ('bumped by a short delay') already implies this, just make it explicit. (2) The proposed fix only covers the retry-item case. The SAME backoff-skip also drops FRESH events: in the block-scan path (request-processor.js:170-178), persistFreshEventsToDurableQueue returns early during the outage (queue.js:277-279) so there is no durable row, processEvent then backoff-skips with retryItem=null (clearRetryItem is a no-op, the event is simply never enqueued locally), and last_block advances (line 178) past the request — so a fresh request discovered during a multi-instance outage is lost with neither a durable record nor a local retry item. A complete fix should also locally enqueue fresh events on backoff_skip. This is an extension, not a defect in the proposed fix.
verified.reason: Confirmed as described by reading the cited code. (1) claimDurableJobForProcessing returns a bare false for two distinct reasons: backoff-skip when allowLocalClaimDuringBackoff=false (queue.js:318-324) and genuine cross-instance conflict (queue.js:382-392). (2) processEvent unconditionally treats false as a conflict: it increments claim_conflicts_total and calls clearRetryItem + persistState with no re-enqueue (fulfillment.js:1040-1051); clearRetryItem filters the item out of retry_queue (state.js:333-336). (3) The retry item IS subject to the backoff-skip: a locally-scheduled callback_retry_scheduled/callback_pending item is enqueued WITHOUT durable_claimed (scheduleCallbackRetry -> enqueueRetryItem, fulfillment.js:880-886; state.js:232 defaults it false). Only Supabase-hydrated items get durable_claimed:true (queue.js:467), which is what short-circuits the claim at queue.js:311. So a local prepared-callback item reaches the backoff-skip on a later tick. (4) No in-outage recovery: hydrateDurableQueue returns [] during backoff (queue.js:397-399), and persistState rewrites the on-disk stateFile (queue.js:30/42) so the item is gone from disk too. (5) Post-recovery recovery is NOT guaranteed: resolveRequestCursor returns lastRequestId+1 (request-processor.js:345) and last_block advances on scan, so once the cursor passes the dropped request id, reconciliation never re-covers it; durable hydration only helps if the row still carries a ready status (the finding's 'durable row may be stale' caveat). Severity high is appropriate-with-caveat: it is a data-loss-class fault on the live oracle callback/money path with no in-outage recovery, but it only fires in a multi-instance deploy with MORPHEUS_DURABLE_QUEUE_ALLOW_LOCAL_CLAIM_DURING_BACKOFF=false during a concurrent Supabase outage. The default/current topology is single-instance (allowLocalClaimDuringBackoff defaults true, config.js:332-335 -> claim granted), so it has not bitten — the finding states this honestly. Not critical because it cannot occur in the default config. Tests (queue.test.mjs:213-235) verify the claim returns false during multi-instance backoff but never exercise the processEvent consequence, so the bug is unguarded. Also note a secondary metric bug the fix correctly removes: backoff-skips are currently miscounted as claim_conflicts_total (fulfillment.js:1041).

verified.title: Neo X (EVM) path has no RPC failover — a single dead RPC endpoint kills the entire neox lane
verified.fix_correction: Fix direction is correct and safe, with one guardrail the implementer must honor (the finding already calls it out): the per-signer nonce serialization (signerKey/submitQueues/NonceManager in neox.js:42-57, 78-89, 379) is keyed on `updaterPrivateKey|rpcUrl`. If fulfillRequest fails over to a different rpcUrl, both the signerCache key and the submitQueue key change, which would break the "one simulate->send->wait per signer" invariant the comments rely on (out-of-order nonces / racy reset()). So: read-path failover (getBlockNumber/totalRequests/getRequest) can rotate freely; the submission path must NOT rotate the URL used for the signer/queue key mid-flight — fall back at the provider level while keeping the signer's queue key stable (e.g. key the queue on the private key alone, or pin the signer to a primary and only swap its underlying provider). The providerCache is already keyed by `rpcUrl|chainId` (neox.js:62) so per-URL providers won't collide. Also add the new array under both neox testnet/mainnet defaults (mirror config.js:511-516) so a default deploy still gets >1 endpoint, matching DEFAULT_NEO_N3_RPC_URLS.
verified.reason: Verified all cited code. neox.js:59-69 getProvider() builds a single ethers.JsonRpcProvider(rpcUrl, chainId) with no FallbackProvider; config.js:511-516 resolves neox.rpcUrl as a single string with no rpcUrls array (the parsed workerUrl at config.js:538 is never imported by the relayer). Every neox read (getNeoXLatestRequestId->readContract->getProvider, getRequest scan at neox.js:234) and the fulfillRequest submission (updaterSigner->getProvider at neox.js:381/397) funnel through that one provider. Contrast confirmed: the N3 path's neoRpcCall (neo-n3.js:68-115) iterates getNeoN3RpcUrls(config) and rotates to the next URL on any error, with a ranked multi-URL list built in config.js:79-110/466-467. The per-tick error handling in request-processor.js (processChainByRequestCursor:287-293) only catches the throw and drains retries — but those retries re-submit through the same dead provider, so a dead/rate-limited neox RPC stalls BOTH discovery and fulfillment with zero recovery, exactly as described. Severity 'high' is defensible: this is a single point of failure on a live production cross-chain delivery path (Neo X mainnet oracle is live per memory), and the N3 lane's existing failover shows the pattern is considered necessary. It is an availability/fault-recovery gap (not an exploitable vuln or fund-loss/correctness bug), which puts it at the high/medium boundary, but the complete absence of failover on a production money/callback lane justifies high.

verified.title: confirmations.neox is dead config in the request-cursor path; a reorged-out Neo X request can be recorded 'settled' (lost callback)
verified.fix_correction: The fix's *direction* is right (gate fulfillment by confirmation depth in processChainByRequestCursor and/or remove the dead config), but its concrete mechanism is factually wrong: the EVM kernel stores createdAt as uint64(block.timestamp), NOT a block number (MorpheusOracleEVM.sol:137). neox.js:175 misleadingly copies record.createdAt into a field named `blockNumber`, but it is a UNIX timestamp. So `provider.getBlockNumber() - createdAtBlock >= confirmations.neox` cannot be computed as written — you would be subtracting a block number from a timestamp. A correct confirmation gate must either: (a) compare the latest block's timestamp against record.createdAt (time-based dwell, e.g. require latestBlockTimestamp - createdAt >= confirmations*avgBlockTime), or (b) resolve the request's actual creation block via the RequestQueued event/log and gate on getBlockNumber() - eventBlock >= confirmations.neox, and critically NOT advance state.neox.last_request_id past ids that are not yet confirmation-deep (defer them to a later tick). The minimal honest alternative (delete confirmations.neox config + RUNBOOK line and document 0-conf) is acceptable only if Neo X finality is truly instant; given dBFT single-block finality that is defensible, but the safer fix is to honor a depth/dwell gate. Also note: any real fix must not regress the neo_n3 block-cursor path (it already gates correctly at request-processor.js:151) and must not break the per-signer serialized submit queue in neox.js.
verified.reason: All code claims verified true. config.js:396 sets confirmations.neox=2; request-processor.js:151 is the ONLY read of config.confirmations[chain] (grep confirms one src reference) and lives in processChain (block-cursor). neox runs unconditionally through processChainByRequestCursor (relayer.js:172-179), which scans/fulfills up to totalRequests() at the tip with no confirmation depth (verified function body 226-302 + neox.js getNeoXLatestRequestId 111-114; no `confirmations` reference in either). The revert->settled chain is code-certain: kernel reverts RequestNotPending when r.status!=Pending (MorpheusOracleEVM.sol:157); a reorged-out id reads back the zeroed struct (status=None), so fulfill/estimateGas reverts RequestNotPending -> normalizeNeoXRevert maps to 'request already fulfilled' (neox.js:318) -> isAlreadyFulfilledError true (fulfillment.js:57) -> classifyError 'settled' (82) -> recordTerminalOutcome durableStatus/retryStatus 'settled', logged info, NO retry (1158-1168). No backstop: reconcilePendingRequests is called only in processChain, never in the request-cursor path; the cursor advances past the id (last_request_id=toRequestId, 284), so a re-created id is never re-scanned. The configuration-trap is also real and operator-facing: RUNBOOK.md:70 advertises MORPHEUS_RELAYER_NEOX_CONFIRMATIONS as an option that does nothing. Severity lowered high->medium: the dead-config/trap is certain, but the lost-callback requires a Neo X reorg that drops an already-committed request tx; Neo X dBFT-style consensus gives effectively single-block finality, making the actual loss path low-probability (the finding itself marks finality needs-verify). fix_ok=false because the proposed createdAt-as-block subtraction is incorrect (createdAt is a timestamp).

verified.title: Production on-chain price feed is single-source (TwelveData) with no cross-source validation
verified.fix_correction: The fix is directionally right (add a second source + median/deviation cross-check, or write one canonical aggregated key) but mis-targets the live path. The actual production mainnet writer is the systemd unit deploy/feed-pusher/feed-pusher.mjs, which does NOT import aggregation.js at all — so hardening feeds.js/handleOracleFeed alone leaves production unchanged. The cross-source check must be added to feed-pusher.mjs itself (the finding's first fix option), OR production push must be switched to the nitro-worker fetchPriceQuotes+aggregation path. The dual-write / legacy-key migration caveat is valid (changing TWELVEDATA:NEO-USD breaks consumers like useMorpheusDataFeed.ts, the admin/price consoles). A simpler, lower-regression mitigation the fix under-weights: add a price-deviation sanity bound in the on-chain MorpheusDataFeed.UpdateFeedInternal (reject a tick deviating > N% from the prior round), which protects ALL writers and consumers without touching storage keys — but cannot catch a sustained wrong-symbol feed, so it complements rather than replaces multi-source. Money/callback/VRF paths are untouched by this code, so no regression concern with the current behavior.
verified.reason: VERIFIED REAL. (1) deploy/feed-pusher/feed-pusher.mjs is the live systemd writer (RUNBOOK.md + morpheus-feed-pusher.{service,timer} confirm a 5-min mainnet timer); its only fetcher is td() at line 123 (TwelveData alone) and every record is keyed 'TWELVEDATA:'+pair (lines 192/246/257). (2) The multi-source engine workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/aggregation.js (median/trimmed-mean + maxDeviationPct outlier rejection) and fetchPriceQuotes (feeds.js:388-430) compute an `aggregation` object, but handleOracleFeed submits one storage_pair PER PROVIDER (feeds.js:584 getFeedStoragePair(quote.provider,...) -> batchUpdates -> submitQuotesToN3 writes entry.storagePair in feed-submit.js:74). The aggregated price is never in batchUpdates; it is only echoed in the HTTP response (feeds.js:751, aggregations object built at 528/580-581). (3) feed-registry.js defines binance-spot only for NEO-USD & GAS-USD (lines 13,18) and getFeedStoragePair (line 306) yields a distinct BINANCE-SPOT:* key. (4) STRENGTHENS the finding: on-chain MorpheusDataFeed.UpdateFeedInternal (MorpheusDataFeed.cs:120-134) does ZERO price-sanity bounding — only price>=0 + roundId/timestamp monotonicity — so a single bad tick is accepted verbatim. Files are on main and recently committed (8838230 'feed-pusher batching'), i.e. current, not stale.\n\nSEVERITY LOWERED high -> medium. The 'lands directly on-chain' claim is true, but the live-money exposure is overstated for this ecosystem: the feed is consumed by display/dashboard code (apps/shared/composables/useMorpheusDataFeed.ts 'Reads prices DIRECTLY from the deployed MorpheusDataFeed'), the admin console, and price consoles — plus any external integrator — but the actual money-path contracts deliberately do NOT read it (e.g. MiniAppSelfLoan.cs:13/21 'NO live oracle', uses an owner-configured neoPrice). No automated on-chain liquidation/borrow decision is wired to this feed today, so a single glitch corrupts what users/integrators see and trust (a genuine oracle-integrity robustness defect) but does not directly drain funds via an in-repo callback. That is medium, not high.

verified.title: Production feed-pusher has no price-sanity / max-deviation guard: zero or absurd source price pushed on-chain
verified.reason:
All cited code claims verified against the source.

CONFIRMED FACTS:
1. planFeedUpdate (feed-pusher.mjs:31-42) only decides push-vs-skip on (a) staleness (cur.round>0 && now-cur.round<maxStaleSec) and (b) a LOWER change threshold (`unchanged` when |move|*10000/cur.price < thresholdBps). There is no upper bound — a 10x spike makes `unchanged=false` so it pushes. Verified.
2. td() (line 137-143): `if (v && !isNaN(Number(v)))` accepts the string "0". Empirically confirmed: string "0" is truthy and Number("0")=0 is finite -> accepted; then Math.round(0*1e6)=0 is pushed (line 248). (Note: a numeric 0 would be rejected by the `v &&` guard, but TwelveData's /price returns price as a string, so the string-"0" case is the realistic one — finding correctly scopes this.)
3. The data flow is unguarded end-to-end: td() -> prices -> pushNeoN3/pushNeoX -> planFeedUpdate -> Math.round(price*1e6) on-chain, with no intermediate sanitization.
4. Contract MorpheusDataFeed.cs:124 only asserts `price >= 0`, so zero/absurd values are accepted on-chain. Comment cs:182-189 acknowledges the gap and offers AdminResetFeed as manual recovery. Verified.
5. The nitro-worker path IS protected as described: transformDecimalString (decimal.js:55-67) throws on numeric<=0, and aggregation.js applies maxDeviationPct outlier rejection. The production cron path (deploy/feed-pusher/feed-pusher.mjs, run by morpheus-feed-pusher.service every 5min via systemd) is a SEPARATE single-source TwelveData pusher with neither guard — confirmed no aggregation/median/deviation code in feed-pusher.mjs.
6. feed-monitor.mjs only checks staleness + gas, NOT price sanity — so there is no out-of-band guard either.

SEVERITY: Corrected high -> medium. It is a genuine robustness/defense-in-depth gap on a real on-chain money/price path, and the production cron path is strictly weaker than the designed multi-source aggregation pipeline. But: (a) not externally attacker-triggerable — it requires a trusted paid provider (TwelveData) to emit "0"/absurd values; (b) admin recovery exists via AdminResetFeed, and the round/timestamp monotonicity guard does NOT block the corrective write (round = max(cur+1, now)); (c) per project memory the MorpheusDataFeed consumer set is small (neo-swap, neo-treasury) and the feed is currently stale/frozen anyway. Medium is the defensible level: real impact if it fires, low likelihood, recoverable.

FIX OK + SAFE: The proposed fix (reject newPrice<=0, !Number.isFinite, or |Δ|/cur.price > MAX_DEVIATION_BPS while cur.price>0, with a bootstrap bypass when cur.price==0; tighten td() to drop v<=0; add tests) is correct and regression-safe. The bootstrap bypass is essential — existing tests at feed-pusher.test.mjs:37-44 assert push=true when cur.price==0, and first-publish requires it. planFeedUpdate is pure and well-tested, so adding rejection branches won't regress the live callback/feed paths; a valid unchanged-but-stale price still force-refreshes since the new rejections only gate invalid/absurd new prices. The finding's own `risk` note honestly flags the operational tradeoff (a legitimate flash move stalls until an admin raises the bound) and recommends an admin-reviewable override — keep that, since otherwise a genuine large move silently stalls the feed.
verified.fix_correction: Fix is sound as written. Two implementation notes to make it complete and safe: (1) The MAX_DEVIATION bypass must trigger on cur.price<=0 (not just ==0), and also when the record is stale and being force-refreshed, so a real large move during a recovery/staleness window is not permanently blocked — pair the deviation rejection with an admin-reviewable override env (e.g. a one-shot ALLOW_DEVIATION_BPS bump) as the finding's risk note recommends, otherwise a genuine >MAX_DEVIATION move stalls the feed until a human intervenes. (2) Apply the same zero/deviation rejection to BOTH chain paths — they each call planFeedUpdate independently (pushNeoN3 line 249, pushNeoX line 376), so centralizing the guard inside planFeedUpdate (rather than only in td()) covers both and also protects the EVM MorpheusPriceFeed lane. Tightening td() alone is necessary but not sufficient for the deviation case.

verified.title: Worker feed pipeline submits unbounded upstream provider timestamps to the strictly-monotonic MorpheusDataFeed, while the production cron uses local-clock-monotonic; a future-dated provider time can permanently stall the on-chain feed until manual AdminResetFeed
verified.fix_correction: The fix is correct and safe; one completeness note: clamp must be applied at ALL three worker submission timestamp derivations, not just feeds.js:607-610. The single-fallback path in feed-submit.js:38-46 (submitQuoteToN3) re-derives the timestamp inline from quote.timestamp with its own floor(Date.parse/1000) and would bypass a clamp added only in feeds.js. The batch path (submitQuotesToN3, feed-submit.js:84) consumes the precomputed entry.timestampSec, so clamping in feeds.js covers it. Cleanest implementation: clamp once at the source where quote.timestamp/observedAt is computed (or add a shared clampSubmissionTimestamp(prevTs, upstreamMs, nowMs, skewSec) helper used by both feeds.js:607 and feed-submit.js:41) so the two on-chain entrypoints cannot diverge. Also consider clamping the lower bound to previousRecord.timestamp+1 rather than just now, to also defend the contract's >= guard against an upstream time slightly behind the last on-chain ts when now has not advanced. Future-skew bound should be generous (>=5min) to tolerate NTP drift as the finding notes.
