(1) MorpheusDataFeed.cs:134 asserts timestamp >= existing.Timestamp — strictly monotonic; AdminResetFeed (line 191) is admin-gated and does NOT re-check the guard, so a future-dated write can only be undone by a human admin — confirms the permanent-stall recovery claim. (2) extractUpstreamTimestamp (feeds.js:209-238) returns the provider datetime/timestamp/time verbatim with only a seconds-vs-ms heuristic and NO now-bound; it becomes quote.timestamp (feeds.js:367); timestampSec = floor(Date.parse(quote.timestamp)/1000) with no clamp (feeds.js:607-610), submitted directly via feed-submit.js:38-46 and :84. (3) feed-pusher planFeedUpdate (feed-pusher.mjs:41) uses ts = Math.max(cur.ts, now) — local-clock monotonic, divergent convention. (4) Key collision is real: getFeedStoragePair (feed-registry.js:307) yields 'TWELVEDATA:<pair>', identical to feed-pusher's 'TWELVEDATA:'+pair; both target the same mainnet contract 0x03013f49... (.env.production.example:88 == RUNBOOK line 9). (5) Both writers share the same enclave updater key, and the worker path submits on-chain whenever CONTRACT_MORPHEUS_DATAFEED_HASH is set — which it IS in .env.production.example and deploy/nitro/morpheus.env.example, both pointed at the live contract, with TWELVEDATA pairs in MORPHEUS_FEED_SYMBOLS. So the two-pipeline collision is operationally reachable, not theoretical. Severity: High is justified — the worst case (future-dated provider time) permanently rejects every subsequent legitimate update until manual admin intervention, breaking downstream price consumers; the failure is data-driven (a single bad provider datetime triggers it). The one caveat keeping it from clearly-critical: the RUNBOOK documents feed-pusher as the standalone sole price writer with a 'single writer invariant', so in the as-operated model the worker /oracle/feed path may not be regularly firing against the same contract — making the live exposure config/usage-dependent rather than guaranteed. That conditionality fits High, not Critical. Fix is correct and matches the feed-pusher's own clamp convention.

verified.title: NeoDID nullifier is derived from an UNVERIFIED, caller-supplied provider_uid for 9 of 10 providers
verified.fix_correction: The fix direction is correct and safe (fail-safe default + per-provider verification/opt-in flag + accurate per-provider derives_provider_uid_in_tee reporting), but two corrections: (1) EXCLUDE zklogin from the scope — handleNeoDidZkLoginTicket (index.js:676-679) already hard-rejects any provider !== 'web3auth', so zklogin tickets are NOT forgeable; the finding's fix/risk text that lumps zklogin in overstates that lane. Only bind, action-ticket, and recovery-ticket are affected. (2) The 'breaking change' risk must account for the fact that the unverified path is currently the DOCUMENTED, TESTED design (apps/web/app/docs/neodid/page.tsx:405-415 shows a binance example with raw provider_uid; worker.test.mjs:406-441 asserts a raw twitter provider_uid succeeds and is deterministic). So a coordinated rollout is mandatory: an env opt-in (default-deny) that ships disabled in the current tests/docs will break the existing test suite and the published API contract for twitter/github/google/discord/telegram/binance/okx/email/generic_oauth. Recommend: implement per-provider verifier adapters where feasible, and for providers without one, gate behind MORPHEUS_NEODID_ALLOW_UNVERIFIED_UID (default off in prod), update docs to mark those providers unverified, and have NeoDIDRegistry/AA-recovery consumers treat unverified-provider nullifiers with reduced trust (or only honor web3auth-rooted recovery).
verified.reason: Verified by reading the cited code directly. CONFIRMED: resolveVerifiedProviderUid (neodid/index.js:181-184) only JWT/JWKS-verifies provider==='web3auth'; all 9 other providers fall through to resolveProviderUid (357-363) returning trimString(payload.provider_uid||social_uid||user_id||account_id) with zero verification. This UID feeds computeMasterNullifier/computeActionNullifier (239-259) — the Sybil/identity-binding primitives — for handleNeoDidBind (533), handleNeoDidActionTicket (565), and handleNeoDidRecoveryTicket (593). The web routes /api/neodid/bind and /api/neodid/recovery-ticket are pure pass-throughs (route.ts forwards raw body); the proxy layer (lib/nitro.ts) adds only an infra x-nitro-token, no OAuth check — so the caller fully controls provider_uid. handleNeoDidRuntime advertises derives_provider_uid_in_tee:true unconditionally (523), only true for web3auth. No MORPHEUS_NEODID_ALLOW_UNVERIFIED_UID opt-in exists anywhere (grep clean) — fail-open is unconditional. DOWNSTREAM IMPACT IS REAL: NeoDIDRegistry.cs (the deployed mainnet morpheus_neodid 0xb81f31ea per config/networks/mainnet.json) blindly trusts the worker's secp256r1 signature + nullifier freshness in RegisterBinding/UseActionTicket — it cannot distinguish a verified from an unverified derivation, and RegisterBinding does not even require CheckWitness(vaultAccount). config/networks/mainnet.json also shows abstract_account (0x0268a387) and aa_verifiers.social_recovery (0x198b3a9c) deployed, so the recovery-ticket (which signs a new_owner) has a live mainnet consumer (its exact trust logic is out-of-repo). SEVERITY high is justified: a forgeable identity-binding/recovery primitive for arbitrary twitter/github/etc identities with a deployed recovery consumer is account-takeover-adjacent. Two caveats pulling toward the high/medium boundary: (a) zklogin is NOT affected — handleNeoDidZkLoginTicket hard-requires web3auth (677-679); the finding overstates by including it; (b) this is the intentional, documented, and unit-tested design (web3auth = 'DID root', other socials linked inside web3auth), not an accidental bug — which is itself the security flaw (insecure-by-design default), and the docs' binance example uses a raw unverified provider_uid. is_real_confidence 'likely' was appropriate; net assessment: real, high.

verified.title: Pre-auth /attestation and /info accept arbitrary caller-controlled report_data with no size/shape limit
verified.fix_correction: Do NOT move /attestation behind requireAuth — that is a regression: it contradicts the documented design contract (README lists GET /attestation as a public Key Endpoint beside /health and /info) and would break the existing passing test worker.test.mjs:1533 ('attestation endpoint works without auth') and any external attestation verifier/monitor that fetches it unauthenticated. Attestation documents are public enclave-identity proof by design; requiring auth defeats their purpose. The fix author even hedges this in their own risk field. The 'at minimum' alternative (cap report_data length + dedicated low rate limit before the enclave call) is safe, but its main premise is already mitigated: server.js:7,14-16 enforce WORKER_MAX_BODY_BYTES (default 256KB) on every request before handler() runs, so the hash input is already bounded. The only genuinely useful hardening left is bounding the per-request unauthenticated outbound enclave /attest round-trip (e.g. a small dedicated rate limit / concurrency cap for the pre-auth attestation path), since acquireOverloadSlot neither runs pre-auth nor recognizes the /attestation route.
verified.reason: Code structure is accurate: worker.js:136-153 handle /health,/info,/attestation before requireAuth (155); /attestation passes report_data|reportData|output_hash|message to buildDstackAttestation->normalizeReportData (nitro-signer.js:218-227) which sha256's arbitrary input and makes an unconditional outbound POST to the enclave /attest (nitro-signer.js:236-241); acquireOverloadSlot runs only after auth (worker.js:160). BUT the finding's central technical premise is factually wrong. (1) 'no size/shape limit' / 'large-JSON hashing at will' is REFUTED: server.js:7,14-16 cap every request body to WORKER_MAX_BODY_BYTES (default 262144 = 256KB) before handler() is ever called, so the sha256 input is bounded to 256KB — the finding's evidence omits this. (2) The pre-auth exposure is intentional and tested, not an oversight: worker.test.mjs:1533 is an explicit passing test 'attestation endpoint works without auth', and README.md:24-26 documents GET /attestation as a public Key Endpoint; attestation material is public enclave-identity proof by design. The only residual real issue is an unauthenticated client driving one localhost enclave /attest round-trip per request with no per-route concurrency cap (each bounded by a 10s AbortSignal, returns null fast when the enclave is unreachable). That residual is a minor amplification at most and infra-frontable; 'low' severity is defensible for it, but the finding as written (arbitrary-size unbounded hashing + treating public exposure as a defect) does not hold, and its primary proposed fix would cause a regression. Net: not confirmed as described.

verified.title: stableStringify sorts keys with locale-default localeCompare (environment-dependent collation on the signing path)
verified.fix_correction: Fix is correct and safe but should be documented as a PARTIAL mitigation. Pinning new Intl.Collator('en', { sensitivity: 'variant', caseFirst: 'false' }) reproduces all 22 golden vectors byte-for-byte (verified) and removes the host LANG/LC_*/OS-locale axis (the dominant, easily-triggered one). However it does NOT remove the ICU-version axis the finding also cites: Intl.Collator and localeCompare share the same per-Node ICU tables, so cross-ICU-version drift for exotic character pairs can still occur. The only fully version-stable ordering is code-unit, which is explicitly forbidden here because live signatures were produced from collation order. So: apply the pinned collator (closes the env-locale hole), keep it gated by STABLE_STRINGIFY_GOLDEN_VECTORS, and note residual ICU-version risk. Also consider adding a guard/test that the signed result envelope only ever contains ASCII snake_case keys, which is what actually makes today's signatures locale-stable in practice.
verified.reason: Confirmed by reading the cited code and empirical testing. FACTS: utils.js:120 sorts with bare a.localeCompare(b) (no locale/Intl.Collator). chain/signing.js:197 hashes stableStringify(result) before signing — genuinely on the live signing path. The worker re-exports the SAME shared function (core.js:2 imports @neo-morpheus-oracle/shared/utils), and relayer/web verifier consume it too, so divergence can only come from runtime collation, exactly as claimed. MECHANISM PROVEN: under LC_ALL=sv_SE.UTF-8, bare localeCompare sorts ['v','w','z','å','ä','ö'] (Swedish: diacritics after z) while pinned Intl.Collator('en') sorts ['å','ä','ö','v','w','z'] regardless of host locale — so bare localeCompare IS host-default-locale dependent and the fix removes that axis. The golden vectors don't expose this because they use only ASCII mixed-case + cross-script unicode (inter-script order is locale-stable); intra-Latin diacritics are where locale matters. FIX VERIFIED: proposed Intl.Collator('en',{sensitivity:'variant',caseFirst:'false'}) reproduces all 22 golden vectors byte-for-byte and is not the forbidden code-unit ordering; existing tests pass at baseline. SEVERITY lowered medium->low: (1) the realistic signed key set is ASCII snake_case (request_id/result/signature/status/pair/price/round_id) which orders identically under bare-locale, pinned-collator, and code-unit across every locale tested — divergence needs non-ASCII keys the current envelope doesn't emit; (2) no live signature is currently failing — this is a latent robustness hazard, not a present correctness break; (3) the fix is only a partial mitigation (removes env-locale axis, not the ICU-version axis the finding itself names). Low/robustness fits better than medium.

verified.title: Dead-lettered jobs are never marked terminal; cron recovery re-requeues permanently-failing jobs forever (infinite loop)
verified.fix_correction: Fix direction is correct and safe. Two refinements: (1) Implement BOTH mechanisms, not either/or. The DLQ-consumer alone does not bound the loop if the DLQs are never created — the wrangler tomls only DOCUMENT the DLQ-create commands in comments, they do not enforce existence; if `morpheus-oracle-request-dlq` is missing, Cloudflare drops/keeps the message but no consumer ever marks the row terminal, so the cron loop persists. A requeue ceiling in recovery.js (track metadata.requeue_attempts on each requeueJob, and once it exceeds a max e.g. 3, patchJob to 'dead_lettered' with completed_at+error instead of re-enqueuing) is the robust guarantee and works for BOTH the non-workflow path and the workflow path (workflow_dispatch_count already exists and is incremented but is never compared to a ceiling — wiring a max onto it is the minimal change). (2) The DLQ consumer handler must read job_id AND network from the DLQ message body and patch with that network (the enqueued/requeued message bodies do carry job_id+network, so this works) — mirror the network-defaulting the existing consumers use (trimString(body.network)==='mainnet'?'mainnet':'testnet'). No DB migration needed: the 0010 CHECK constraint already permits 'dead_lettered'. Marking 'dead_lettered' removes the row from listRecoverableJobs because that query filters status=in.(queued,processing,failed,dispatched) and the consumers' TERMINAL_JOB_STATUSES set already includes 'dead_lettered', so any still-parked main-queue message also self-acks — no regression to live fulfillment/callback/feed paths, which are protected independently by the terminal-status guard, the non-stale-processing skip, and the dedupe_key unique index.
verified.reason: Confirmed real by reading all cited code. (1) worker.mjs queue() (249-261) routes only 'morpheus-oracle-request' and 'morpheus-feed-tick'; any other queue (incl. the two DLQs) falls through to message.ack() — no DLQ consumer exists. (2) wrangler.meshmini.toml binds dead_letter_queue=morpheus-oracle-request-dlq (33) and morpheus-feed-tick-dlq (40) but declares no [[queues.consumers]] for them. (3) Exhaustive grep confirms 'dead_lettered' is NEVER assigned anywhere: it appears only in the TERMINAL_JOB_STATUSES set (queue-consumer.js:11), the DB CHECK constraint (0010_control_plane_jobs.sql:24), the TS type union (packages/shared/src/index.ts:20), and two read-only terminal checks in scripts — there is no patchJob(...status:'dead_lettered'...) in the codebase. (4) Cron */5 (toml:45) -> scheduled -> recoverNetworkJobs -> listRecoverableJobs (recovery.js:8-44) re-lists non-terminal queued/processing/(workflow failed/dispatched) rows; requeueJob (recovery.js:54-93) patches status->'dispatched' and enqueues a BRAND-NEW message into the main producer queue with fresh attempts, and there is NO requeue ceiling (grep for any comparison on workflow_dispatch_count/requeue_attempts returned empty; the count is stored but never gated). Loop trace: a job that keeps hitting retryable execution-plane errors (isRetryableStatus: 408/409/425/429/5xx, or thrown fetch errors) exhausts max_retries=10 -> Cloudflare routes the message to a consumer-less DLQ (parked) while the Supabase row is left at 'queued' (the last patch before the final message.retry) -> next cron tick re-lists it (run_after elapsed + >REQUEUE_GRACE_MS 60s) -> fresh message -> 10 more retries -> DLQ -> repeats every 5 min indefinitely, burning queue + Phala/edge execution-plane resources. README:67-68 claims the DLQ makes loss 'inspectable instead of silent', which is misleading since the live row neither resolves nor stops. Severity corrected high->medium: it is a genuine unbounded resource-waste/state-pollution loop and the terminal dead-letter state is truly never reached, BUT it is NOT money/correctness-affecting — duplicate fulfillment/double-callback is independently prevented by the consumer's terminal-status guard, the non-stale 'processing' skip, and the dedupe_key unique index; it only fires under sustained retryable-failure (outage) conditions and self-heals once upstream recovers, and genuinely non-retryable failures DO terminate ('failed'+ack, non-workflow 'failed' is never re-listed). Operational blast radius (cost, log/queue backlog) rather than financial -> medium. Minor scope nit: the finding's subsystem label includes morpheus-edge-gateway, but that worker has no queues/scheduled handler at all — the defect is entirely in morpheus-control-plane. Relevant files: deploy/cloudflare/morpheus-control-plane/worker.mjs, wrangler.meshmini.toml, lib/recovery.js, lib/queue-consumer.js, lib/jobs.js, lib/config.js, lib/workflows.js, README.md; supabase/migrations/0010_control_plane_jobs.sql.

verified.title: Turnstile body-token path can consume the request body before it is forwarded to origin (prefixed POST routes)
verified.fix_correction: Fix direction is correct and safe; I validated two variants that preserve the origin body (buffer body once via request.clone().text() then parse the token and forward the original untouched; OR derive turnstileRequest from a clone() of the original so new Request(url, request) ties the copy, not the original). Either works; cloning the original directly is safe. IMPORTANT scope correction for the fix: the lock is caused by new Request(url, request) at worker.mjs:413 combined with the UNCONDITIONAL request.clone() at worker.mjs:150 — NOT by cloned.json(). So restricting Turnstile to header-only tokens does NOT fix it (the clone still locks the original); the header path breaks too. The fix must avoid tying the turnstile request to the original body stream entirely (buffer/clone approach), not just change where the token is read. Add the requested regression test: a network-prefixed turnstile-protected POST (e.g. /testnet/paymaster/authorize) with TURNSTILE_WORKER_SECRET set, asserting the origin still receives the full body — and add a header-token variant too.
verified.reason: Confirmed real and reproduced end-to-end through worker.fetch (Node/undici): with Turnstile enabled on a network-prefixed protected POST (/testnet/paymaster/authorize), forwarding throws `TypeError: Response body object should not be disturbed or locked` at buildOriginRequest (worker.mjs:252) called from worker.mjs:447. Root cause: new Request(`${url.origin}${forwardedPath}${url.search}`, request) at lines 411-414 ties the original request's body stream, and verifyTurnstile's unconditional request.clone() at line 150 then disturbs/locks the original; buildOriginRequest forwards the original request.body at line 255. Two corrections vs the finding: (1) the trigger is BROADER than 'token in JSON body' — isolation tests show the lock comes from new Request(url,request)+clone, not from .json(), so the HEADER-token case breaks too (line 150 clone is unconditional). It fires on ANY prefixed turnstile-protected POST when Turnstile is configured and the request is untrusted. (2) Impact is WORSE than 'silently corrupts the body' — buildOriginRequest (line 447) is OUTSIDE the origin-fetch try/catch (starts line 453) and fetch has no outer handler, so the TypeError is UNCAUGHT and the client gets a Worker exception (HTTP 500/1101), not the graceful 503. Severity medium is right: gated on the optional TURNSTILE_WORKER_SECRET/TURNSTILE_SECRET_KEY (README.md:26 'optional', not in committed prod wrangler config), so latent unless an operator enables Turnstile — but when enabled it is a full outage of the live-money paymaster/relay/vrf/compute POST paths. The non-prefixed/root path (turnstileRequest = request, line 414) and the Turnstile-disabled path are unaffected (verified), and the existing oracle-feed POST test (worker.test.mjs:181) cannot catch it (no Turnstile secret, /oracle/feed not in TURNSTILE_PROTECTED_PATHS, and it does not assert the origin body). Could not test against the real workerd runtime (no wrangler/miniflare installed), but workerd implements the same Fetch 'disturbed or locked' body semantics and is generally stricter than undici, so the mechanism holds.

verified.title: Quickstart "seal your parameters" step calls a non-existent function (encryptWithOracleX25519) while a real working helper ships in the codebase
verified.fix_correction: The proposed fix is correct and safe. The optional "promote to packages/shared" step is reasonable: packages/shared/src/confidential-envelope.js already implements the identical scheme (encryptConfidentialEnvelope at line 187, ENVELOPE_ALGORITHM/INFO match exactly), so the cleanest published-package option is to document `encryptConfidentialEnvelope` from a shared export rather than inventing a new shared name.

(1) apps/web/app/docs/quickstart/page.tsx:113 and apps/web/app/docs/oracle/page.tsx:205 both contain `encryptWithOracleX25519(JSON.stringify(secrets), public_key)` inside CodeBlock snippet strings. verified.title: Sealed-envelope wire format is never documented, so the encryption step is unreproducible without reading source
verified.reason:
REFUTED. The core claim — that the envelope wire format "is never documented" and is "unreproducible without reading source" / a non-JS developer "cannot construct a valid ciphertext" — is false. The repo contains comprehensive, git-tracked documentation of exactly this format:

1. docs/HPKE_X25519_MIGRATION.md (git-tracked) has a dedicated "Envelope Format" section (lines 52-74) documenting the full JSON schema with every field name (v, alg, epk, iv, ct, tag) AND each field's encoding/byte size (<base64 ephemeral X25519 public key>, <base64 12-byte AES-GCM nonce>, <base64 16-byte GCM tag>), AND the outer "UTF-8 encoded and base64 wrapped" step, AND which request fields carry it (encrypted_payload/params/input/token). It also gives a language-agnostic 7-step "Browser Encryption Flow" and "Worker Decryption Flow" (lines 91-109) and the /oracle/public-key response shape.

2. docs/EXAMPLES.md (git-tracked) Sections 3 and 4 (lines 190-329) provide complete copy-pasteable browser AND Node.js reference implementations showing the HKDF info string ('morpheus-confidential-payload-v2' + epk + recipient pubkey), salt=recipient pubkey, 12-byte IV, 16-byte tag split, and base64(JSON) wrapping.

3. packages/shared/src/confidential-envelope.js is an explicitly-labeled "single reference implementation" with a 37-line header documenting the exact wire format, HKDF salt/info binding, and byte lengths; it is dependency-free specifically so it "can be vendored verbatim where a workspace import is not possible."

4. examples/browser-encryption/encrypt-oracle-payload.js is a standalone working example.

So every item the fix requests (field names, encodings, byte sizes, HKDF salt/info, base64(JSON) wrapping) already exists in docs. The HKDF info binding the finding says is undocumented is spelled out in confidential-envelope.js lines 13-26 and reconstructable from EXAMPLES.md lines 228-232.

There is a much smaller real gap (not what was reported): the apps/web RENDERED docs pages (quickstart/page.tsx line 113, oracle/page.tsx line 205) reference a helper encryptWithOracleX25519(...) that is not defined/exported anywhere (the real export is encryptJsonWithOraclePublicKey in lib/browser-encryption.ts), and those specific web pages neither inline the schema nor cross-link to docs/HPKE_X25519_MIGRATION.md or docs/EXAMPLES.md. That is a discoverability/dead-reference nit on the web docs surface, severity low at most — not a high-severity "never documented / unreproducible" issue. The evidence's load-bearing assertion is factually wrong, the severity is overstated, and the proposed fix duplicates documentation that already exists.
verified.fix_correction: The premise of the fix (the schema must be added because it is undocumented) is wrong — the schema is already fully documented in docs/HPKE_X25519_MIGRATION.md ("Envelope Format" + flow sections) and docs/EXAMPLES.md (Sections 3-4), and is the canonical comment header of packages/shared/src/confidential-envelope.js. The only legitimate (low-severity) DX improvement is on the apps/web docs pages themselves: (1) fix the dead reference encryptWithOracleX25519(...) in app/docs/quickstart/page.tsx (line 113) and app/docs/oracle/page.tsx (line 205) since no such helper is exported (the real one is encryptJsonWithOraclePublicKey in apps/web/lib/browser-encryption.ts); and (2) add a cross-link from those pages (or api-reference/page.tsx) to docs/HPKE_X25519_MIGRATION.md / docs/EXAMPLES.md / the packages/shared reference module so the existing spec is discoverable. No need to author a new schema section.

verified.title: Public HTTP API (rate-limited REST endpoints) is essentially undocumented in the API Reference
verified.fix_correction: Fix is correct and safe (additive docs only, zero code-path risk). So the new HTTP API section should consolidate/cross-link those existing pieces rather than duplicate them, and focus net-new documentation on the genuinely undocumented core endpoints: POST /api/oracle/query (30/min), POST /api/oracle/smart-fetch (20/min), POST /api/compute/execute (20/min), GET /api/feeds/[symbol], plus the cross-cutting X-RateLimit headers and the degraded/unavailable fallback shapes — none of which appear anywhere in the docs tree (verified by grep).
verified.reason: Confirmed against source. (1) All five endpoints are wrapped in createRateLimitedHandler with X-RateLimit-* headers: oracle/query maxRequests 30 (route.ts:78), smart-fetch 20 (:78), compute/execute 20 (:57), feeds/status 30 (:19), confidential/store 20 (:187); headers set in lib/rate-limit.ts:148-161. (2) Degraded/fallback shapes confirmed at feeds/[symbol]/route.ts:19-37 and oracle/public-key/route.ts:45-60. (3) docs/api-reference/page.tsx covers ONLY the Neo N3 Contract.Call pattern + GET /api/neodid/resolve — zero REST oracle/feeds/compute endpoints, exactly as claimed. (4) No OpenAPI/Swagger file or tooling anywhere in repo (verified repo-wide; public/ has no spec; keyword hits are incidental 'scalar' prose). (5) The core money/data-path endpoints (oracle query, smart-fetch, compute execute, feeds price), their rate limits, X-RateLimit headers, and degraded shapes are documented NOWHERE in the docs tree (grep empty). ACCURACY CORRECTION: the literal claim 'docs list ZERO REST endpoints' is scoped to the api-reference page and overstated for the whole docs surface — docs/neodid/page.tsx does document NeoDID REST routes and POST /api/confidential/store with examples. SEVERITY: downgraded high->medium. Real and worth fixing, but documentation-only with no security/money/callback/feed runtime impact, partial coverage already exists, and the most sensitive public endpoint (confidential/store) is in fact already documented. 'High' overstates a pure docs-completeness gap.

verified.title: Canonical encryption primitive (encryptConfidentialEnvelope) is not exported from @neo-morpheus-oracle/shared, forcing every integrator to copy it
verified.fix_correction: Fix is correct and behavior-neutral, with two refinements. (1) Mirror the existing subpath-export shape and ship types: the canonical module is .js with no co-located .d.ts (unlike ./utils which ships utils.d.ts), so add `"./confidential-envelope": { "types": "./src/confidential-envelope.d.ts", "default": "./src/confidential-envelope.js" }` plus a new confidential-envelope.d.ts, otherwise TS consumers get no types. (2) packages/shared is `"private": true`, so this does NOT enable true external/npm import as the evidence states ("external consumers cannot import...") — it only helps intra-monorepo importers and clarifies the vendoring source. The drift-guard replacement claim should be verified during implementation, not assumed: the named `sync_morpheus_registry` guard does not exist in this repo (only feed-registry-drift guards do), so collapsing copies to imports is sound but isn't actually protected by an in-repo guard today. Golden-vector test (confidential-envelope.test.mjs) pins the wire bytes, so adding exports cannot change behavior — risk note is accurate.
verified.reason: All core factual claims verified against the code. packages/shared/package.json:6-14 has no ./confidential-envelope export; neither index.js nor index.ts re-exports encrypt/decrypt/parse/generate from confidential-envelope.js; the file's header (lines 1-37) does call itself the "single reference implementation" written "dependency-free so it can be vendored verbatim"; and the two cited copies exist verbatim at examples/scripts/common.mjs:430-492 and examples/browser-encryption/encrypt-oracle-payload.js:25-87. The canonical module is in fact imported by NOTHING except its own test — even the worker decryptor (workers/nitro-worker/src/oracle/crypto.js) and apps/web/lib/browser-encryption.ts re-implement it inline; counting message-reveal.test.mjs and runtime-service-matrix.mjs there are ~6 in-repo copies, not the "~3" stated. So the duplication problem is real and slightly understated. Two inaccuracies in the finding: (a) the "guarded by a drift checker" framing — the comment names a sync_morpheus_registry guard that does not exist in this repo, so the in-repo copies are effectively UNguarded (this strengthens the concern but the cited evidence is wrong); (b) "external consumers cannot import" overstates impact since the package is private:true. Severity downgraded high->medium: this is a pure DX/maintainability issue (duplicated security-sensitive crypto with documented-canonical-but-unexportable source) with ZERO functional impact on live money/callback/feed/decryption paths — the wire format is already pinned by golden vectors and all paths work via copies today.

verified.title: Quickstart and oracle docs reference a function name (encryptWithOracleX25519) that exists nowhere in the codebase
verified.fix_correction: Fix is correct and safe (docs-only, no runtime/contract/callback/feed code touched). Two refinements: (1) Pick ONE canonical name and apply it to BOTH docs files consistently — recommend referencing encryptConfidentialEnvelope (packages/shared/src/confidential-envelope.js:187, already exported) since it is the canonical, tested helper, or encryptWithOracleKey which is the actual shipped example helper. (2) IMPORTANT — also fix the argument order, not just the name: the docs call (JSON.stringify(secrets), public_key) i.e. (plaintext, publicKey), but the real helpers take publicKey first: encryptWithOracleKey(publicKeyBase64, plaintext) and encryptConfidentialEnvelope(recipientPublicKey, plaintext). Renaming alone without swapping argument order would still produce a broken/misbehaving snippet. Best: make the snippet self-contained (inline the helper or an import line) or link directly to examples/browser-encryption/encrypt-oracle-payload.js.
verified.reason: Verified all factual claims directly. encryptWithOracleX25519 appears exactly at apps/web/app/docs/quickstart/page.tsx:113 and apps/web/app/docs/oracle/page.tsx:205 inside runnable CodeBlock snippets, with no import or local definition anywhere in the repo (grep excluding node_modules/.next returns only those two usages, zero definitions). The actual shipped helpers are encryptWithOracleKey (examples/browser-encryption/encrypt-oracle-payload.js:25, examples/scripts/common.mjs:430) and the canonical exported encryptConfidentialEnvelope (packages/shared/src/confidential-envelope.js:187). A developer copy-pasting the quickstart gets a ReferenceError — confirmed; the rest of the snippet is otherwise runnable (the /api/oracle/public-key endpoint exists at apps/web/app/api/oracle/public-key/route.ts), so the bad function name is the sole break point. Severity corrected from high to medium: this is a DX/documentation copy-paste defect with no impact on live money/callback/feed paths, and working alternatives ship in the repo. Additional unstated wrinkle: the docs also use the wrong argument order (plaintext, publicKey) vs the real helpers (publicKey, plaintext), so the fix must address both name and arg order.

verified.title: Example encryption helpers don't check the degraded `available:false` body before encrypting with a null oracle public key
verified.reason:
All cited code is accurate. apps/web/app/api/oracle/public-key/route.ts:9-60 returns HTTP 200 with {available:false, degraded:true, public_key:null} on upstream 401/403/408/429/5xx. examples/browser-encryption/encrypt-oracle-payload.js:1-7 only checks response.ok (true for the degraded 200), and lines 89-97 then pass the null public_key into encryptWithOracleKey -> decodeBase64ToBytes(null) -> atob(null). The quickstart snippet (app/docs/quickstart/page.tsx:104-105) reproduces the no-check pattern. The node example is correctly noted as unaffected (it reads the key on-chain via common.mjs:393-424 and throws on empty/non-ok).

CORRECTION to the failure mechanism, which lowers severity: the finding claims the result is "an encrypted blob bound to a key nobody holds, submitted on-chain (fee paid), undecryptable." That is not what happens. I tested it: atob(null) coerces to the string "null" and decodes to a 3-byte buffer; crypto.subtle.importKey('raw', <3 bytes>, {name:'X25519'}) THROWS DataError ("X25519 raw keys must be exactly 32-bytes") per the WebCrypto Secure-Curves spec (verified in Node webcrypto; browsers enforce the same 32-byte requirement). So the helper throws before producing any ciphertext — there is no silently-undecryptable blob and no wasted on-chain fee from this path. The actual defect is a confusing crash (cryptic crypto error instead of a clear "oracle unavailable" message) during known degraded windows.

Severity high is too high: (1) this is example/docs-only code, not a shipped runtime path; (2) the real production component StarterStudio.tsx already guards correctly (line 144 stores key only if body?.public_key, line 284 throws if !keyMeta?.public_key); (3) the outcome is a thrown exception, not money loss. This is a low-severity robustness/DX bug in example helpers.
verified.fix_correction: The proposed guard (if (!body.available || !body.public_key) throw new Error('oracle public key unavailable: ' + (body.message||body.error))) is correct and safe: it mirrors the guard already used in StarterStudio.tsx, touches only example/docs code, and does not affect the live money/callback/feed paths. Keep the proxy's degraded-200 contract as-is (intentional; callers rely on the `available` flag) and fix the example/quickstart callers, exactly as the finding recommends. Minor note: the error string should reflect that the throw is the primary mitigation regardless of whether a blob would otherwise be malformed or rejected at importKey — but the guard itself is right.

verified.title: Caller-supplied `signing_key` private-key material is logged in cleartext (web operation-log redaction misses it)
verified.reason:
Core vulnerability verified true. operation-logs.ts:36 SENSITIVE_KEY_PATTERN does NOT match `signing_key` (empirically: node test returns false), yet `signing_key` is a real caller-supplied private-key alias consumed by the worker (signing.js:25,132,194 and neo-n3.js:92 use `payload.signing_key` directly to build a neoWallet.Account and sign). Both admin routes /api/sign/payload (route.ts:54) and /api/relay/transaction (route.ts:55) forward the full parsed body as requestPayload to recordOperationLog — on the 400/401 paths AND on the authorized success path via proxyToNitro (nitro.ts:154-172). sanitizeValue (operation-logs.ts:98-114) only redacts on immediate-key match, so {signing_key:"Kx...WIF..."} is persisted verbatim to morpheus_operation_logs.request_payload (line 235) and mirrored to the external BetterStack sink (line 255 -> betterstack-log-sink.ts). The worker's own error redaction (core.js:274-284) DOES include /signing[_ ]?key/i, confirming the web omission is an oversight, not intentional.

ONE OVERSTATEMENT in the finding (does not negate it): the claim that `mnemonic`, `seed`, and `passphrase` are "first-class caller-supplied private-key fields accepted by the worker everywhere" is FALSE — grep shows zero consumption of mnemonic/seed/passphrase anywhere in workers/nitro-worker/src. Only `signing_key` is a real active vector (alongside the already-redacted private_key/wif). So actual current exposure is the single signing_key field, not the broader vocabulary. Adding the others is still good defensive hardening, just not closing an active hole.

SEVERITY: high is appropriate. A spendable Neo private key written in cleartext to the operations DB (Supabase) AND an external third-party log sink (BetterStack) is a genuine key-exfiltration path on the highest-value admin (sign/relay) routes; DB-read or log access yields a spendable key. Likelihood is tempered by the need for the caller to use the `signing_key` alias (vs the already-redacted private_key/wif), and `signing_key` is not referenced in apps/web or docs as a public alias — but it is a code-accepted equivalent. High (not critical) is the right call.

FIX: correct and safe. Verified empirically that adding signing[_-]?key|mnemonic|seed[_-]?phrase|seed|passphrase|credential|signing_seed redacts all target secret fields (signing_key, signingKey, etc. -> true) while leaving every legitimate non-secret field that flows through the live feed/callback/sign paths untouched (public_key, oracle_public_key, key_role, key_source, key_version, key_format, verification_public_key, sponsor_public_key all stay false). The bare `seed` and `credential` tokens are broad but harmless given no legitimate seed/credential-named non-secret fields exist in payloads (no over-redaction regression). The shared-redaction-list recommendation is sound DRY hardening to prevent vocabulary drift.
verified.fix_correction: Fix is correct and safe as written. Minor refinements: (1) The active hole is only `signing_key` — mnemonic/seed/passphrase are NOT consumed by the worker, so prioritize `signing[_-]?key` (the proven vector); the rest are defensive. (2) Prefer matching `signing_key` via word-aware boundary or keep the broad token — verified no current over-redaction either way. (3) Strongly endorse the single shared redaction list in @neo-morpheus-oracle/shared imported by both apps/web/lib/operation-logs.ts and workers/nitro-worker/src/platform/core.js to prevent the exact drift that caused this. (4) Add the unit test asserting {signing_key, signingKey} redact while {public_key, oracle_public_key, key_role} do not, to lock in no-over-redaction on the live feed/sign paths.

verified.title: Dead-lettered (permanently lost) oracle callback fires no alert hook — only a log line + counter
verified.reason: Verified against source. recordDeliveryExhaustion (fulfillment.js:783-846) is the terminal dead-letter path and does exactly three observable things: incrementMetric(state,'retries_exhausted_total') (794), recordProcessedEvent(...'exhausted') + persistState (795-811), maybeUpsertJob status 'exhausted' (813-821), and one logger.error 'Callback delivery retries exhausted; dead-lettered...' (823-838). No sendHeartbeat call anywhere in the function — confirmed by grep (the only sendHeartbeat imports/usages in the worker are relayer.js:200/206/256). All three call sites (1197 callback-delivery exhausted, 1234 failure-finalize exhausted, 1323 finalize-of-failure exhausted) are genuinely terminal: a request whose callback will never be delivered. The relayer failure heartbeat config.heartbeats.failure (config.js:548, MORPHEUS_BETTERSTACK_RELAYER_FAILURE_URL) is fired ONLY from the whole-tick crash catch at relayer.js:256 — never on dead-letter. A repo-wide search confirms no other push-alert path covers relayer dead-letters; the Cloudflare control-plane DLQ (wrangler dead_letter_queue) is a separate queue-transport mechanism, not the relayer's per-request exhaustion path. So the claim 'the single most important incident produces no dedicated push alert; operator learns only via counter delta or log grep' is accurate. Severity high is defensible for an oracle whose core SLA is callback delivery: permanent delivery loss with no real-time alert materially slows incident response. (A medium argument exists — metric + durable 'exhausted' status + structured log do exist, so detectability is degraded rather than absent, and an operator could wire an alarm on retries_exhausted_total — but high is the right call given the impact.)
verified.fix_correction: Fix is correct and safe with one prerequisite the finding already names. sendHeartbeat (heartbeat.js:11-32) is already best-effort fire-and-forget: it returns false on an empty/blank URL and swallows all fetch errors under a 3s abort timeout, so an unconfigured dead-letter URL is a no-op — no money/callback/feed behavior change. Must add the new config key (config.js:545-549 heartbeats block) e.g. deadLetter: env('MORPHEUS_BETTERSTACK_RELAYER_DEADLETTER_URL') since config.heartbeats.deadLetter does not exist yet, and document the env var (docs/ENVIRONMENT.md:494, .env*.example, deploy/nitro compose + render-nitro-compose-env.mjs:496 area for parity). Fire it void (not awaited) to preserve the non-blocking pattern, placed at the end of recordDeliveryExhaustion (after maybeUpsertJob, before the return) so the durable state is already persisted; the proposed 'after persistState()' is also fine since it's fire-and-forget. Payload fields (chain, request_id, request_type, module_id, operation, attempts, error) are all already in scope (they mirror the existing logger.error object at 824-836). Falling back to config.heartbeats.failure when the dedicated URL is unset is reasonable but means dead-letters and tick crashes would hit the same monitor — acceptable as a default; routing to a separate higher-severity monitor (PagerDuty) is the intended end state.

verified.title: No delivery-latency or queue-age metric — operator cannot distinguish 'slow but recovering' from 'permanently stuck'
verified.fix_correction: Fix targets the correct functions and is safe (pure read-side aggregation; no write/callback/feed path change). One implementation caveat: first_failed_at (state.js:222,311) and exhausted_at (state.js:272) are ISO-8601 STRINGS, not epoch-ms, so the literal `now - min(first_failed_at)` must Date.parse() them first (reuse prometheus.js's existing parseIsoToUnixSeconds helper / the timestamp-gauge pattern at prometheus.js:57-69). next_retry_at is the numeric field; first_failed_at is not. With that correction the three proposed gauges (oldest_retry_age_seconds{chain}, oldest_dead_letter_age_seconds{chain}, last_fulfill_latency from requestAgeMs at completion) are implementable as described.
verified.reason: VERIFIED REAL, every citation accurate. fulfillment.js:1011-1014 computes requestAgeMs; it is only logged (1034,1107,1149) and stored to processed-record meta (1107) — never recorded as a metric (grep of all src/ confirms no incrementMetric/metric for it). state.js snapshotMetrics (166-186) emits retry_queue_sizes/dead_letter_sizes as .length COUNTS only (174-175), no age. prometheus.js (71-79) exports those counts only; no latency/age gauge anywhere. retry_queue[].first_failed_at and dead_letters[].exhausted_at exist but are never aggregated. /metrics (metrics-server.js:11) serves exactly renderPrometheusMetrics(snapshotMetrics(...)). The one nearby mechanism, healthcheck.js, measures TICK-LOOP LIVENESS (last_tick_completed_at age), NOT delivery latency — so it does not cover this gap; a relayer can tick green while a specific callback is wedged for hours. This corroborates the finding's thesis. SEVERITY DOWNGRADED to medium: this is a missing-observability/degraded-detectability gap, not a defect on the live money/callback/feed path. Funds and delivery still work; the retry+dead-letter machinery already handles stuck callbacks (dead-letter, manual replay), and the existing dead_letter_size{chain} count plus tick-liveness healthcheck already give a coarse alertable 'something is permanently stuck' signal. The proposed gauges improve MTTD/SLO precision but their absence is not an outage, correctness, or security issue, so 'high' overstates it relative to the real money/correctness findings in this repo.

verified.title: Relayer systemd unit has no liveness probe — a wedged-but-alive tick loop is never restarted
verified.reason:
Core claim verified against the cited code. morpheus-relayer.service uses Type=simple + Restart=always + RestartSec=5 (lines 7,11,12) — these restart only on process EXIT, never on a hung-but-alive loop. The same holds for morpheus-relayer-nitro.service and morpheus-nitro-worker.service. healthcheck.js (checkRelayerStateFreshness) detects last_tick_completed_at staleness and exits non-zero (lines 55-62); it IS wired into the docker path (docker-compose.request-hub.yml:161-166 and :200-205) but is NOT referenced by any systemd unit — a repo-wide grep found zero WatchdogSec / Type=notify / sd_notify. The systemd deploy is a documented production pattern (docs/RELAYER.md:141-154). last_tick_completed_at is set only at the end of a successful tick (relayer.js:184), runRelayerOnce awaits chain scans with no OUTER tick-level timeout (relayer.js:135), and runRelayerLoop (line 229) only catches THROWN errors, so a never-resolving await is invisible — confirming the wedged-loop scenario. Docker restart:unless-stopped also does not act on 'unhealthy' without autoheal, as noted.

Two accuracy caveats (do not invalidate the finding): (1) The evidence's literal claim that "a stuck RPC fetch inside a tick hangs the loop indefinitely" is overstated — the in-repo chain I/O is actually bounded: neoRpcCall uses AbortController + RPC_TIMEOUT_MS=30s (neo-n3.js:76-77), n3index calls use AbortSignal.timeout(30_000) (lines 206,460), NeoX waitForNeoXReceipt uses a Promise.race deadline (neox.js:341-358), and the confirm for(;;) loop has a deadline guard (neo-n3.js:1002). So a stuck RPC fetch specifically times out rather than hangs forever. The structural gap is still real (timeouts are per-call/opt-in, don't cover every path, and cumulative sequential retries within one tick can still exceed the 120s freshness window; future/uncovered code or an AbortSignal-ignoring provider could wedge). (2) Severity is better rated medium than high: the single most likely hang vector is already mitigated by per-call timeouts; the gap is on the documented systemd path while the primary Nitro production path runs under docker which already has the probe; and this is a recovery-resilience / defense-in-depth gap, not an active money/correctness defect. It is a real, worth-fixing parity gap.
verified.fix_correction: Both proposed fixes are correct and safe with no regression to the live money/callback/feed paths (both are external/supervisory). The oneshot+timer-runs-healthcheck.js-then-systemctl-restart variant is the lower-risk option since it reuses healthcheck.js's existing correct exit codes (lines 57-61) and needs zero application-code changes. If choosing the Type=notify + WatchdogSec + sd_notify WATCHDOG=1-per-tick variant, WatchdogSec must be tuned well above worst-case tick duration: with 30s RPC timeouts multiplied across retries and multiple chains, a legitimate slow tick can exceed the default 120s freshness window, so a too-tight WatchdogSec would cause flapping. Either way, the fix should also be applied to morpheus-relayer-nitro.service (the primary Nitro systemd variant), not just morpheus-relayer.service.

verified.title: request_age_ms is meaningless on the Neo N3 path because createdAtMs may be a block height aliased to blockNumber
verified.fix_correction: The fix's main branches are moot because the premise (possible block-height) is false. createdAtMs is already a verified epoch-ms timestamp, so 'derive from block timestamp / set null if it is a block index' never applies. The only defensible part is the cosmetic 'stop aliasing blockNumber to createdAtMs on the record path' (set 0 or a real height), but this has zero functional or observability impact since event.blockNumber is consumed only by buildEventKey dedup (which stays stable because createdAtMs is deterministic per requestId), persistence.js:380, and state.js:258 audit fields — never as a chain height for cursor advancement. The test-assertion suggestion is harmless hygiene but guards a non-existent risk. No code change is required for correctness.
verified.reason: REFUTED. The finding's core premise — that createdAtMs 'may be a block height' making request_age_ms garbage — is factually wrong. Contract proof: MorpheusOracle.cs:1340 sets KernelRequest.CreatedAt = Runtime.Time; the contract's own doc comment (line 119) states 'Runtime.Time on Neo N3 is in milliseconds since epoch'; and line 682 computes age = Runtime.Time - req.CreatedAt compared to DEFAULT_REQUEST_TTL = 3_600_000 (1 hour in ms), which is on-chain proof of the epoch-ms unit. The relayer's 14-element decode (neo-n3.js:252) reads struct field index 9 (CreatedAt) from the serialized KernelRequest returned by getRequest — field order matches exactly. Therefore request_age_ms = Date.now() - createdAtMs (fulfillment.js:1013) is correct, not 'absurd'. Additionally, the only path that populates createdAtMs is the record-read path (buildNeoN3EventFromRequestRecord); the DEFAULT live scan modes (n3index_notifications testnet / block_cursor mainnet, config.js:446) use buildNeoN3EventFromNotificationState which never sets createdAtMs, so requestAgeMs correctly resolves to null there. The secondary blockNumber-alias observation is technically accurate but is a cosmetic mislabel with no functional or dedup impact (event.blockNumber is never used as an actual chain height for cursor advancement; cursor advances by request id on this path). Severity 'low' overstates it; the metric works as designed, so this is best classified not-a-bug.

verified.title: Neither oracle contract caps the signed result to its on-chain MAX_RESULT_LENGTH before signing — latent stuck-request risk
verified.fix_correction: The fix direction (assert result/error length before signing, fail fast to a finalize-failed result) is safe and non-regressive, but refine it: (1) Put the guard at the single chokepoint signFulfillmentPayload (fulfillment.js:250) — every lane (default/vrf/automation/decrypt) calls it, whereas guarding only in encodeFulfillmentResult/router.js would MISS the confidential.decrypt lane (fulfillment.js:680-714), which is precisely the one that bypasses the 900/384/255-byte caps and signs raw plaintext. (2) Apply the 4096 result cap ONLY for chain==='neo_n3'; the EVM oracle (MorpheusOracleEVM.sol:153 fulfillRequest) has NO result-length cap, so an EVM guard at 4096 would needlessly reject legitimately large EVM results. Compute the byte length from result_bytes_base64 (decoded) when present, else Buffer.byteLength(result). (3) error cap <=256 matches N3 MAX_ERROR_LENGTH; ensure the replacement finalize-failed error itself satisfies the cap (it will — it's a short literal).
verified.reason: Verified the cited code. CONFIRMED real-but-narrow latent gap: N3 contract MorpheusOracle.cs:926 asserts result.Length<=MAX_RESULT_LENGTH(4096) BEFORE signature verification at line 943, while the off-chain signer buildFulfillmentDigestBytes (router.js:302) and its caller signFulfillmentPayload (fulfillment.js:250) hash the result with no length cap — so an oversized result yields a valid signature whose FulfillRequest reverts 'result too large', leaving the request Pending until the 1h TTL ExpireStaleRequest (refundable, no fund loss). TWO inaccuracies in the finding's framing weaken it: (1) Title overclaims 'neither oracle contract' — the EVM oracle MorpheusOracleEVM.sol has NO MAX_RESULT_LENGTH at all (fulfillRequest accepts arbitrary bytes, no revert), so the EVM half cannot strand anything; the 4096 cap is N3-only. (2) The finding missed an upstream N3 invariant: the riskiest lane (confidential.decrypt, fulfillment.js:680-714, which bypasses the 900/384/255-byte mitigations and signs raw plaintext) is on N3 fed by the request payload, which is itself capped at MAX_PAYLOAD_LENGTH=4096 (MorpheusOracle.cs:1113); the decrypted plaintext = ciphertext minus AEAD/X25519 overhead, hence strictly <4096B on N3, so it cannot trigger the bug. (EVM maxEnvelopeBytes defaults 8192, up to 65536, but EVM has no result cap.) The JSON-envelope lane's 900-byte selection (router.js:575-581) does fall back to the last candidate even if >900B, but compactEnvelope/buildBusinessResult copy only fixed small scalar fields + sources.slice(0,4) + compactValue<=384B, so reaching 4096B is implausible. Net: real defensive-depth gap, not triggerable on either live chain today, no money/feed/callback regression — 'low' is appropriate (borderline very-low/not-a-bug). The proposed off-chain belt-and-suspenders fix is correct and safe with the refinements noted (N3-only result cap, place at signFulfillmentPayload to cover the decrypt lane).
